The roofline tooth was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.

Her mouth did the metallic thing again—like someone had pressed a coin to the back of her tongue.

She swallowed it because there was nowhere to put it.

That taste was getting old.

“Okay,” she told herself, but quiet, because saying it loud would make it a lie people could hear. “Okay.”

She didn’t cry.

Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn’t decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet, and she didn’t want to start and not be able to stop.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.

A man passed carrying a basket of broken brick. He said, “Watch your feet, kid,” without looking at her.

Chloen watched her feet anyway.

The stone underfoot wasn’t steady in the way it used to be. It was steady like a tired person holding their face still so no one asked questions. Every few breaths the doubled hum ran up through her shoes, and the grit on the ground did a tiny lift-settle like it was answering something she couldn’t hear.

She imagined the city as a giant animal trying not to shiver.

She didn’t like that either.

She turned her head so she didn’t have to keep staring at the gap where her home had been.

People talked around her like she wasn’t there. Grown-ups did that when they didn’t want to be careful.

“—said the whole quarter folded,” a woman whispered, voice tight. “Just… sank and set wrong.”

“Not folded,” someone argued back. “Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth.”

“Entry was the ocean,” a man said, too loud. “My cousin’s out on the coast. If he’s alive, he’ll tell you—”

“You don’t know it was the ocean,” another snapped. “You don’t know anything.”

Chloen listened anyway, because listening was how you survived grown-ups.

“—the sky’s still open,” someone murmured, and everyone who heard that line went quiet for a beat like they’d all looked up without wanting to.

“Stars in the day,” an old man said, and spat into the dust. “End-times.”

“Not end-times,” a brace captain barked nearby. “Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked.”

A child cried. A baby, too little to understand the difference between hunger and fear.

Chloen wanted to go find the older woman who’d wiped her face earlier and ask for the cloth again. The cloth felt like a promise that skin could be clean.

She didn’t move.

If she moved, she might drift toward that gap. If she drifted toward the gap, someone might ask where her people were.

Where’s your aunt, Chloen?

And if she answered, she would have to say: probably under a roof that doesn’t exist anymore.

So she stood still and practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One. A man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn’t want anyone to see it.
Two. A cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.
Three. Rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.
Four. A pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.

The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the whole crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.

When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she’d been taught and hated that she’d been taught anything today.

She heard Tiffani’s voice before she saw her.

Not loud. Not comforting. Just clear.

“Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped.”

Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.

Tiffani was a few paces away in the semicircle that had broken into work groups. Slate tucked under her arm again. She stood like the ground was an instrument and she didn’t trust it.

Jhace was near her, wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.

“Someone moved the ocean,” Jhace said, and even though Chloen had already watched it happen, hearing him say it made her stomach go cold again.

Someone else answered—one of the important men with rings, maybe, or one of the brace captains. Chloen couldn’t see who. She could hear the shape of the words.

“Not possible.”

“It happened,” Jhace said.

Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and then they might see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.

Tiffani said, “We measure,” and Chloen didn’t know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn’t fix.

Jhace’s voice lowered. “And if the slate woman shows up again—”

Slate woman.

Chloen’s skin prickled. The woman with chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace’s hands.

Chloen didn’t like her. She didn’t know her. She didn’t like the way the woman’s attention had felt like fingers.

Tiffani’s pencil tapped once. A small sound. A decision sound.

Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones, even though it had fallen and broken and was done.

She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.

The bend in the terrace wall. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.

Her throat tightened.

She kept it to herself.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn’t bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people, and today was already full of wrong.

Chloen stared at the skyline until her eyes watered, then blinked hard and looked away before the water could turn into something else.

“Okay,” she told herself again, quieter.

Then, because she didn’t know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn’t believe, but needed anyway.

“Still here.”

And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.

Not crying.

Not speaking.

Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.
EOF

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the noise thinned enough that words wouldn’t get eaten by it.

It found him in the gap between jobs—after the semicircle meeting broke into lines and chalk marks, after the factor’s attendants started handing out orders to people already doing the work, after the brace captains moved off to rope cracks and post lookouts.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm. Not writing. Watching the dust drift with the pressure pulses like it had learned a new tide. Chloen stayed a step behind her, small and rigid, eyes moving constantly.

Jhace came up with a wedge bar in his hand because setting tools down today felt like asking for them back broken.

“Who are you?” he said.

Chloen flinched. Tiffani didn’t.

“Tiffani,” she answered. No title. No explanation. A label.

Jhace nodded once. “You live up here?”

“Above the high terrace,” she said. “Glass-house.”

Observatory, then. That accounted for the way she treated catastrophe like a ledger.

Jhace looked down at the girl.

“And you.”

Chloen lifted her chin like stubbornness was armor. “Chloen.”

He repeated it to lock it. “Chloen.”

“Good,” he said—flat, practical. Alive was a status.

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on the horizon. “You’re Jhace.”

He glanced at her.

“I heard it,” she added, already anticipating the bite.

“Yeah.”

A pressure pulse rolled through the parapet. The stone underfoot carried two notes—old and new. Dust lifted, settled. The fine copper-gold grit at the edge of Tiffani’s slate rose a hair and fell again.

Jhace’s mouth went metallic. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

He kept his gaze on the worklines and said, “There’s a woman. Slate. Chalk. Counting.”

Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched—recognition of method, not person.

“I saw her twice,” Jhace said. “Once after the slab. Once before the fire line. She wasn’t watching the Rift. She was watching my hands.”

Chloen’s eyes cut to him, then away. Kids noticed watchers.

“Describe her,” Tiffani said.

“Dust cloak. Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it’s keeping her upright.”

Tiffani nodded once. “Not mine,” she said. “Not from the glass-house.”

“Do you know who she is?”

“I know what she is,” Tiffani said. “A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn’t crack.”

Jhace stared at the city’s broken skyline. The idea sat wrong and familiar at the same time.

“That means it won’t stay private,” he said.

“No,” Tiffani agreed.

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar until his knuckles went pale. The tremor in his fingers tried to rise. He pinned it with pressure.

“If she shows up again,” he said, “you tell me.”

Tiffani didn’t answer with words. She tapped her pencil once against the slate—controlled. A marker.

Chloen edged closer, into the space between them as if their bodies were a windbreak.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum kept sitting under everything like a second heartbeat.

For the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the two bodies nearest him—names he could use when stone shifted and seconds mattered.

---

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn’t like looking up.

Looking up was how you found the missing parts.

She stood on the wide stone flat where people passed rubble hand to hand, where voices turned into lists and directions, where the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through cloth. She wrapped her arms around herself because her sleeves were still damp in places and her skin didn’t feel like it belonged to her.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself—terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs that pretended they were streets. The city always looked like it had been built by someone who hated level ground.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Chloen found the place her home had been.

Not by signs. Signs were gone or turned sideways.

By the bend in a terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.

Her rooms had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One stubborn window. Aunt Lessa’s pot hooks by the stove. The chipped bowl they kept even though Aunt said it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.

The crooked tooth roofline was gone.

A gap sat there instead—stone slumped, dust filling the space where walls used to decide things.

Her throat tightened.

She didn’t cry. Not because she was brave. Because she didn’t want anyone asking questions she couldn’t answer without making it real.

The metallic taste hit her again. Like a penny held too long against the tongue.

She swallowed it.

That taste was getting old.

“Okay,” she told herself—quiet, so it wouldn’t turn into a lie other people could hear.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Hurt was simple.

Around her, grown-ups talked like she was furniture.

“—quarter folded,” a woman whispered. “Just sank and set wrong.”

“Not folded,” someone argued back. “Rang. I felt it in my teeth.”

“Entry was the ocean,” a man said, too loud. “My cousin’s out there—”

“You don’t know anything,” another voice snapped.

Someone said, “Sky’s still open,” and a hush dropped for a beat, like everyone had looked up without wanting to.

“Stars in the day,” an old man muttered, and spat.

A brace captain barked, “Off the cracks I marked,” and people moved because that was a command you could obey.

Chloen stayed still. If she drifted toward the gap up there, someone would ask where her people were.

Where’s your aunt, Chloen?

And she’d have to say: probably under a roof that doesn’t exist anymore.

So she practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One: a man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn’t want anyone to notice.

Two: a cart turned into a supply table with cups lined up neat.

Three: rope pulled tight across a seam in the stone, biting like it meant business.

Four: slate fragments in a pile, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.

The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.

When it slacked, she filled her lungs the way the grown-ups were teaching each other to do. She hated that she’d learned anything today.

She heard Tiffani’s voice—clear, not loud.

“Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped.”

Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.

Tiffani stood near the parapet, slate under her arm again. Jhace was close by with his wedge bar, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.

“Someone moved the ocean,” Jhace said.

Chloen’s stomach went cold again even though she’d already watched it happen.

A man with rings—important—answered from somewhere nearby. “Not possible.”

“It happened,” Jhace said.

Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.

Tiffani said, “We measure,” like measuring was the only way to keep the world from being all teeth.

Jhace’s voice dropped. “And if the slate woman shows up again—”

Slate woman.

Chloen’s skin prickled. The chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace’s hands.

Tiffani’s pencil tapped once—a small, controlled sound.

Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the slab above her in her bones even though it had fallen and broken and was done.

She looked up again, fast.

The bend in the wall. The missing roofline. The gap.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

She kept it to herself.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn’t bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people.

“Okay,” she told herself again.

Then, because she didn’t know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn’t believe, but needed anyway.

“Still here.”

And she stayed on the stone flat where names were called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she trusted today.

Not crying.

Not speaking.

Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.
EOF

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the crowd noise thinned enough that words didn’t get swallowed by it.

It happened in a pocket between tasks—after the meeting broke into work assignments, after the factor’s people started pretending they were in charge again, after the brace captains peeled off to mark cracks and post rope lines.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm, not writing, just watching dust lift and settle on the pressure pulses. Chloen hovered a step behind her, close enough to share shadow without touching.

Jhace came up on their left with a wedge bar still in his hand. Putting it down felt like tempting the stone.

He didn’t soften his voice.

“Who are you?”

Chloen flinched. Tiffani shifted her weight to spare her knee without making it look like surrender.

“Tiffani,” she said. No title, no explanation. Like labeling a jar.

“You live up here?”

“Above the high terrace,” she said. “Glass-house.”

Jhace filed it away. Observatory. Instruments. The kind of mind that kept a slate alive when the sky tore.

He looked down at the girl.

“And you.”

Her chin lifted a fraction, stubborn reflex. “Chloen.”

He nodded once. “Chloen.”

“Good,” he said, and meant: good you’re still breathing.

Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the horizon. “You’re Jhace.”

He looked at her.

“I heard it,” she added.

“Yeah.”

A pulse rolled through the parapet stone. Copper-gold grit along the edge of her slate lifted a hair and settled again. The taste hit Jhace’s mouth—metallic again, thin and sharp. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

“There’s a woman,” he said. “Slate. Chalk. Counting.”

Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched at the word counting. Not interest—recognition.

“I saw her twice,” Jhace went on. “After the slab. Again before the fire line. She wasn’t looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands.”

Chloen’s eyes flicked between them. She had noticed that kind of watching too.

“Describe her,” Tiffani said.

“Dust cloak,” Jhace said. “Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it keeps her upright.”

Tiffani nodded once. “Not mine,” she said. “Not from the glass-house.”

“Do you know who she is?”

“I know what she is,” Tiffani said. “A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn’t break.”

Jhace stared past her shoulder at the worklines—hands passing stone, rope going up, chalk marks spreading across the flats. Someone else was already trying to make a system out of what had happened.

He didn’t like it. He understood it.

“That’s three of us,” he said, more to himself than them. “One who holds. One who measures. One who survives.”

Chloen’s mouth tightened. “I didn’t—”

“I know,” Jhace cut in. “You did.”

She stopped. Swallowed. That taste was getting old for him, and he hadn’t been the one under the stone.

Tiffani’s eyes went back to the horizon. “The others will start asking,” she said.

“Let them,” Jhace said. His fingers flexed once on the wedge bar, then stilled. “If that slate woman shows up again—”

Tiffani’s pencil tapped the slate once. A small, controlled sound.

“—you tell me,” Jhace finished.

Tiffani didn’t answer with words. She didn’t need to.

Chloen edged closer to the space between them. Jhace didn’t move away.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum sat under everything like a second heartbeat.

For the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the bodies next to him—names he could use when stone decided to fall again.

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn’t like looking up.

Looking up was how you saw what wasn’t there anymore.

She stood on the wide stone flat where rubble moved hand to hand, where voices had turned into lists, where the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through cloth. She wrapped her arms around herself because her sleeves were still damp in places and her skin didn’t feel like it fit right.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself—terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs that pretended they were streets. The city always looked like it had been built by someone who hated level ground.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Corners that used to be straight leaned. A whole section of parapet was missing. New scars cut across terraces where stone had slid and then stopped. Smoke rose from three places. One column was darker than the others.

Chloen found the line where her street had been.

Not by a sign. Signs were gone or turned sideways.

By the bend in the terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.

Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One small window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa’s pot hooks by the stove. A shelf with the chipped bowl they kept even though it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.

Chloen stared at the bend.

The roofline tooth was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.

The metallic hit her again, like someone pressed a coin to the back of her tongue.

She swallowed because there was nowhere to put it.

“Okay,” she told herself, but quiet, because saying it loud would make it a lie other people could hear. “Okay.”

She didn’t cry.

Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn’t decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet, and she didn’t want to start and not be able to stop.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.

A man passed carrying a basket of broken brick. “Watch your feet, kid,” he said without looking at her.

Chloen watched her feet anyway.

The stone under her wasn’t steady in the way it used to be. It was steady like a tired person holding their face still so no one asked questions. Every few breaths the doubled hum ran up through her shoes, and the grit on the ground did a tiny lift-settle like it was answering something she couldn’t hear.

She didn’t like that either.

She turned her head so she didn’t have to keep staring at the gap.

Grown-ups talked around her like she wasn’t there. They did that when they didn’t want to be careful.

“—said the whole quarter sank,” a woman whispered. “Just set wrong.”

“Not sank,” someone argued. “Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth.”

“Entry was the ocean,” a man said too loud. “My cousin’s on the coast. If he’s alive, he’ll tell you—”

“You don’t know it was the ocean,” another snapped. “You don’t know anything.”

Chloen listened anyway, because listening was how you survived.

“—the sky’s still open,” someone murmured, and everyone who heard it went quiet for a beat like they’d all looked up without wanting to.

“Stars in the day,” an old man said, and spat into the dust. “End-times.”

“Not end-times,” a brace captain barked nearby. “Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked.”

A child cried. A baby, too little to know the difference between hunger and fear.

Chloen wanted to go find the older woman who’d wiped her face earlier and ask for the cloth again. The cloth had felt like a promise that skin could be clean.

She didn’t move.

If she moved, she might drift toward the gap. If she drifted toward the gap, someone might ask where her people were.

Where’s your aunt, Chloen?

And if she answered, she would have to say: probably under a roof that doesn’t exist anymore.

So she stood still and practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One: a man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn’t want anyone to see it.
Two: a cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.
Three: rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.
Four: a pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.

The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too, because the air told her to.

When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she’d been taught and hated that she’d been taught anything today.

She heard Tiffani’s voice before she saw her.

“Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped.”

Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.

Tiffani stood a few paces away, slate tucked under her arm again. She stood like the ground was an instrument and she didn’t trust it.

Jhace was near her, wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.

“Someone moved the ocean,” Jhace said.

Chloen’s stomach went cold again.

Someone answered—an important man, or a brace captain. She couldn’t see who. She could hear the shape of it.

“Not possible.”

“It happened,” Jhace said.

Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.

Tiffani said, “We measure,” and Chloen didn’t know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn’t fix.

Jhace’s voice lowered. “And if the slate woman shows up again—”

Slate woman.

Chloen’s skin prickled. The chalk. The counting. The eyes.

Tiffani’s pencil tapped once. Controlled. Decision sound.

Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones even though it had fallen and broken and was done.

She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.

The bend in the terrace wall. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.

Her throat tightened.

She kept it to herself.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn’t bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people.

She stared at the skyline until her eyes watered, then blinked hard and looked away before the water could turn into something else.

“Okay,” she told herself again, quieter.

Then, because she didn’t know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn’t believe, but needed anyway.

“Still here.”

And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.

Not crying.
Not speaking.
Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the crowd noise thinned enough that words didn’t get swallowed by it.

It happened in a pocket between tasks—after the meeting broke into work assignments, after the factor’s people started pretending they were in charge again, after the brace captains peeled off to mark cracks and post rope lines.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm, not writing, just watching the way the pressure pulses pushed dust into slow drifts and then pulled it back. Chloen hovered a step behind her like she’d decided proximity was safer than distance.

Jhace came up on their left, wedge bar still in his hand because putting it down felt like tempting fate.

He didn’t soften his voice. There wasn’t time to dress it.

“Who are you?” he asked.

Chloen flinched like he’d snapped at her. Tiffani didn’t. She shifted her weight to spare her knee without letting it look like a concession.

“Tiffani,” she said. No preamble. No apology. Like she was labeling a specimen jar.

Jhace nodded once, filed it away. “You live up here?”

“Above the high terrace,” she said. “Glass-house.”

Observatory, then. That explained the slate, the chronometer in her head, the way she looked at the Rift like it was a problem that could be measured into submission.

Jhace looked down at the girl.

Chloen’s chin lifted a fraction, stubborn reflex. Her hands were still dirty. She’d stopped wiping them like it mattered.

He didn’t ask gently.

“And you.”

She blinked, then said, “Chloen.”

The name sat wrong in his mouth for a beat—too soft for the day it had been forced into.

He nodded anyway. “Chloen.”

He let the wedge bar rest against his thigh. The tremor in his fingers tried to start up and he ignored it by giving his hands something to do.

“Good,” he said, and meant: good you’re alive.

He didn’t add the rest. He didn’t know how to say it without making it sound like ownership.

Tiffani’s eyes were still on the horizon. “You’re Jhace,” she said.

He looked at her.

“I heard it,” she added, because she knew how that sounded.

Jhace’s jaw worked. “Yeah.”

A pressure pulse rolled through the parapet stone. Dust lifted. Copper-gold grit along the slate edge rose a hair and settled again.

Jhace watched it happen and got the metallic hit at the back of his tongue like a reminder. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

He forced his attention away from his own body and back onto the practical.

“There’s a woman,” he said. “Slate. Chalk. Counting.”

Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched once at the word counting. Not interest—recognition.

“I saw her twice,” Jhace went on. “Once after the slab. Once again before the fire line. She wasn’t looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands.”

Chloen’s gaze flicked between them. She’d seen that kind of watching too. Kids noticed hunters.

Tiffani finally looked at Jhace. “Describe her.”

“Dust cloak,” he said. “Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it’s keeping her upright.”

Tiffani held that for a beat, then nodded once. “Not mine,” she said. “Not from the glass-house.”

“Do you know who she is?” Jhace asked.

“I know what she is,” Tiffani said, and her voice went flatter. “A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn’t break.”

Jhace stared past her shoulder at the worklines forming—hands passing stone, names being called, rope being tied. He let the implication settle where it wanted.

Someone else was already trying to make a system out of what had happened.

He didn’t like that. He also understood it.

“That’s three of us,” Jhace said, more to himself than them. “One who holds. One who measures. One who survives.”

Chloen’s mouth tightened. “I didn’t—”

“I know,” Jhace cut in, not unkind. “You did.”

She stopped. Swallowed. That taste was getting old, even for him, and he hadn’t been the one under the slab.

Tiffani’s gaze went back to the horizon. “The others will start asking,” she said.

“Let them,” Jhace said. His hands flexed once, then stilled on the wedge bar. “But if that slate woman shows up again—”

Tiffani’s pencil tapped her slate once. Controlled. A marker.

“—you tell me,” Jhace finished.

Tiffani didn’t answer with words. She didn’t need to.

Chloen edged closer to the space between them, and Jhace didn’t move away.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum sat under everything like a second heartbeat.

And for the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the bodies next to him—names he could shout if stone decided to fall again.

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn’t like looking up.

Looking up was how you saw what wasn’t there anymore.

She stood on the wide stone flat where people kept passing rubble hand to hand, where voices had turned into lists and directions, where the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through a cloth. She stood with her arms wrapped around herself because her sleeves were still damp in places and her skin didn’t feel like it fit right.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself—terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs that pretended they were streets. The city always looked like it had been built by someone who hated level ground.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Corners that used to be straight leaned. A whole section of parapet was missing. There were new scars where stone had slid and then stopped as if it had remembered rules mid-fall. Smoke rose from three places. One column was darker than the others.

Chloen found the line where her street had been.

Not by a sign. Signs were gone or turned sideways.

By the bend in the terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.

Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One small window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa’s pot hooks by the stove. A shelf with the chipped bowl they kept even though it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.

Chloen stared at the bend.

The roofline tooth was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.

Her mouth did the metallic thing again—like someone had pressed a coin to the back of her tongue.

She swallowed it because there was nowhere to put it.

That taste was getting old.

“Okay,” she told herself, but quiet, because saying it loud would make it a lie people could hear. “Okay.”

She didn’t cry.

Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn’t decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet, and she didn’t want to start and not be able to stop.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.

A man passed carrying a basket of broken brick. He said, “Watch your feet, kid,” without looking at her.

Chloen watched her feet anyway.

The stone underfoot wasn’t steady in the way it used to be. It was steady like a tired person holding their face still so no one asked questions. Every few breaths the doubled hum ran up through her shoes, and the grit on the ground did a tiny lift-settle like it was answering something she couldn’t hear.

She imagined the city as a giant animal trying not to shiver.

She didn’t like that either.

She turned her head so she didn’t have to keep staring at the gap where her home had been.

People talked around her like she wasn’t there. Grown-ups did that when they didn’t want to be careful.

“—said the whole quarter folded,” a woman whispered, voice tight. “Just… sank and set wrong.”

“Not folded,” someone argued back. “Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth.”

“Entry was the ocean,” a man said, too loud. “My cousin’s out on the coast. If he’s alive, he’ll tell you—”

“You don’t know it was the ocean,” another snapped. “You don’t know anything.”

Chloen listened anyway, because listening was how you survived grown-ups.

“—the sky’s still open,” someone murmured, and everyone who heard that line went quiet for a beat like they’d all looked up without wanting to.

“Stars in the day,” an old man said, and spat into the dust. “End-times.”

“Not end-times,” a brace captain barked nearby. “Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked.”

A child cried. A baby, too little to understand the difference between hunger and fear.

Chloen wanted to go find the older woman who’d wiped her face earlier and ask for the cloth again. The cloth felt like a promise that skin could be clean.

She didn’t move.

If she moved, she might drift toward that gap. If she drifted toward the gap, someone might ask where her people were.

Where’s your aunt, Chloen?

And if she answered, she would have to say: probably under a roof that doesn’t exist anymore.

So she stood still and practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One. A man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn’t want anyone to see it.
Two. A cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.
Three. Rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.
Four. A pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.

The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the whole crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.

When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she’d been taught and hated that she’d been taught anything today.

She heard Tiffani’s voice before she saw her.

Not loud. Not comforting. Just clear.

“Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped.”

Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.

Tiffani was a few paces away in the semicircle that had broken into work groups. Slate tucked under her arm again. She stood like the ground was an instrument and she didn’t trust it.

Jhace was near her, wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.

“Someone moved the ocean,” Jhace said, and even though Chloen had already watched it happen, hearing him say it made her stomach go cold again.

Someone else answered—one of the important men with rings, maybe, or one of the brace captains. Chloen couldn’t see who. She could hear the shape of the words.

“Not possible.”

“It happened,” Jhace said.

Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and then they might see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.

Tiffani said, “We measure,” and Chloen didn’t know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn’t fix.

Jhace’s voice lowered. “And if the slate woman shows up again—”

Slate woman.

Chloen’s skin prickled. The woman with chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace’s hands.

Chloen didn’t like her. She didn’t know her. She didn’t like the way the woman’s attention had felt like fingers.

Tiffani’s pencil tapped once. A small sound. A decision sound.

Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones, even though it had fallen and broken and was done.

She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.

The bend in the terrace wall. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.

Her throat tightened.

She kept it to herself.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn’t bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people, and today was already full of wrong.

Chloen stared at the skyline until her eyes watered, then blinked hard and looked away before the water could turn into something else.

“Okay,” she told herself again, quieter.

Then, because she didn’t know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn’t believe, but needed anyway.

“Still here.”

And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.

Not crying.

Not speaking.

Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.
EOF

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the noise thinned enough that words wouldn't be eaten by it.

It found him between jobs—after the semicircle meeting broke apart into lines and chalk marks, after the factor's attendants started repeating orders people were already doing, after brace captains moved off to post rope across cracks.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm. She wasn't writing. She was watching. The copper grit along the slate edge lifted and settled in time with the pressure pulses.

Chloen hovered a step behind her, small and quiet, eyes tracking hands, boots, falling dust.

Jhace came up on their left with his wedge bar still in hand because putting it down felt like tempting the city.

"Who are you?" he asked.

Chloen flinched. Tiffani didn't.

"Tiffani," she said. No title. No explanation. Like a label on a jar.

"You live up here?" Jhace asked.

"Above the high terrace. Glass-house." Her knee hitched once. She shifted her weight and didn't let it show.

Jhace looked down at the girl.

"And you."

Chloen lifted her chin a fraction, stubborn.

"Chloen," she said.

He nodded once. "Good."

Not comfort. A tally. Alive.

Tiffani's eyes flicked to him. "You're Jhace."

He looked at her.

"I heard it," she added, as if that made it less invasive.

"Yeah," he said.

The metallic taste hit the back of his tongue again. Not a surge. A reminder. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

He kept his hands low and went back to the practical.

"There's a woman," he said. "Slate. Chalk. Counting."

Tiffani's pencil hand twitched at the word counting.

"Saw her twice," Jhace said. "After the slab. Again near the fire line. She wasn't looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands."

"Describe her," Tiffani said.

"Dust cloak. Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it's keeping her upright."

Tiffani held that for a beat and shook her head once. "Not mine. Not from the glass-house."

"Do you know who she is?" Jhace asked.

"I know what she is," Tiffani said. "A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn't break."

Jhace stared past them at the worklines: stone passed hand to hand, rope being tied, names being called.

"If she shows up again," he said, "you tell me."

Tiffani's pencil tapped her slate once. Controlled. A marker.

Chloen edged closer to the space between them. Jhace didn't move away.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum sat under everything like a second heartbeat.

Jhace exhaled on the slack phase and tasted metal again.

At least now he had names he could use when the next wall decided to fail.

---

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn't like looking up.

Looking up was how you saw what wasn't there anymore.

She stood on the wide stone flat where people passed rubble hand to hand and called names until voices went raw. The air pulsed thin and then thick again, like breathing through cloth. She wrapped her arms around herself because her sleeves were damp in places and her skin didn't feel like it fit.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself: terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs pretending to be streets. It had always looked like someone built it because they hated level ground.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Corners that used to be straight leaned. A parapet section was simply missing. Smoke rose from three places, one column darker than the others.

Chloen found where her street had been.

Not by a sign. Signs were gone or turned sideways.

By the bend in the terrace wall and the crooked roofline that used to look like a tooth.

Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa's pot hooks by the stove. A chipped bowl they kept because replacing it cost more than pretending it was fine.

Chloen stared at the bend.

The roofline tooth was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself back in with dust.

The metallic taste came again, like someone pressed a coin to the back of her tongue.

She swallowed. There was nowhere to put it.

That taste was getting old.

"Okay," she told herself. Quiet. Saying it out loud would make it a lie other people could hear.

She didn't cry.

Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn't decided what sound it wanted to make, and she didn't want to start and not be able to stop.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.

Grown-ups talked around her like she wasn't there. They did that when they didn't want to be careful.

"Whole quarter folded," a woman whispered. "Just sank and set wrong."

"Not folded," someone argued. "Rang. I felt it in my teeth."

"Entry was the ocean," a man said, too loud. "My cousin's on the coast. If he's alive he'll tell you."

"You don't know," another snapped. "You don't know anything."

"Sky's still open," someone murmured, and the nearby voices went thin for a beat like everyone looked up without wanting to.

"Stars in the day," an old man said and spat into the dust. "End-times."

"Not end-times," a brace captain barked. "Not if you keep off the cracks I marked."

A baby cried. A child cried harder. The sound scraped.

Chloen kept her eyes down and counted instead.

One: a man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn't want anyone to see it.

Two: a smashed cart turned into a supply table with cups lined up in a neat row.

Three: rope tied across a crack, pulled tight enough it bit into stone.

Four: slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.

When the pull came, the crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to. When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she'd been taught and hated that she'd been taught anything today.

She heard Jhace and Tiffani before she saw them.

"Someone moved the ocean," Jhace said, and even though she'd watched it happen, hearing him say it made her stomach go cold again.

"Not possible," someone answered.

"It happened," Jhace said.

Tiffani's voice was level. "We measure."

Jhace lowered his voice. "And the slate woman."

Slate woman.

Chloen's skin prickled. The chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace's hands.

She didn't like her. She didn't know her. She didn't like the way that attention felt like fingers.

Chloen looked up again, quick, like touching a bruise.

The bend in the wall. The missing tooth. The gap.

Her throat tightened.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

She kept it to herself.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn't bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people, and today was already full of wrong.

Chloen blinked hard and looked away before the water in her eyes could turn into something else.

"Still here," she whispered to herself.

Then she stayed on the stone flat where names were called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.

Not crying.

Not speaking.

Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.
EOF

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the crowd noise thinned enough that words didn’t get swallowed by it.

It happened in a pocket between tasks—after the meeting broke into work assignments, after the factor’s people started pretending they were in charge again, after the brace captains peeled off to mark cracks and post rope lines.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm, not writing, just watching the pressure pulses push dust into slow drifts and then pull it back. Chloen hovered a step behind her like she’d decided proximity was safer than distance.

Jhace came up on their left, wedge bar still in his hand because putting it down felt like tempting fate.

He didn’t soften his voice.

“Who are you?” he asked.

Chloen flinched like he’d snapped at her. Tiffani didn’t. She shifted her weight to spare her knee without letting it look like a concession.

“Tiffani,” she said. No preamble. No apology. Like she was labeling a jar.

Jhace nodded once, filed it away. “You live up here?”

“Above the high terrace,” she said. “Glass-house.”

Jhace looked down at the girl.

“And you.”

Chloen’s chin lifted a fraction. Her hands were still dirty. She’d stopped wiping them like it mattered.

“Chloen,” she said.

“Good,” Jhace said, and meant good you’re alive.

Tiffani’s eyes stayed on the horizon. “You’re Jhace.”

He looked at her.

“I heard it,” she added.

Jhace’s jaw worked. “Yeah.”

A pressure pulse rolled through the parapet stone. Dust lifted and settled. The copper-gold grit along the slate’s edge rose a hair and dropped again.

Jhace’s mouth went metallic. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

“There’s a woman,” he said. “Slate. Chalk. Counting.”

Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched once at the word counting.

“I saw her twice,” Jhace went on. “Once after the slab. Once before the fire line. She wasn’t looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands.”

Chloen’s gaze flicked between them. Kids noticed hunters.

“Describe her,” Tiffani said.

“Dust cloak,” Jhace said. “Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it’s keeping her upright.”

Tiffani held it for a beat, then nodded once. “Not from the glass-house.”

“Do you know her?” Jhace asked.

“I know the type,” Tiffani said. “Recorder. Registrar. Or someone building a frame so she doesn’t break.”

Jhace stared past them at the worklines forming—hands passing stone, names being called, rope being tied. The implication settled where it wanted.

Someone else was already trying to make a system out of what had happened.

“That slate woman shows up again,” Jhace said, “you tell me.”

Tiffani didn’t answer with words. Her pencil tapped the slate once. Controlled. A marker.

Chloen edged closer to the space between them. Jhace didn’t move away.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum kept sitting under everything like a second heartbeat.

And for the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the bodies next to him—names that would matter the next time stone decided to fall.

---

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn’t like looking up.

Looking up was how you saw what wasn’t there anymore.

She stood on the wide stone flat where people passed rubble hand to hand, where voices turned into lists and directions, where the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through cloth. Her sleeves were damp in places and her skin didn’t feel like it fit right.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself—terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs pretending they were streets. The city always looked like it had been built by someone who hated level ground.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Corners that used to be straight leaned. A whole section of parapet was missing. There were new scars where stone had slid and then stopped as if it had remembered rules mid-fall. Smoke rose from three places.

Chloen found the line where her street had been.

Not by a sign. Signs were gone or turned sideways.

By the bend in the terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.

Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One small window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa’s pot hooks by the stove. A shelf with the chipped bowl they kept even though it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.

Chloen stared at the bend.

The roofline tooth was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.

Her mouth did the metallic thing again. Like someone had pressed a coin to the back of her tongue.

She swallowed because there was nowhere to put it.

That taste was getting old.

“Okay,” she told herself, but quiet, because saying it loud would make it a lie people could hear. “Okay.”

She didn’t cry.

Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn’t decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet, and she didn’t want to start and not be able to stop.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.

A man passed carrying a basket of broken brick. “Watch your feet, kid,” he said without looking at her.

Chloen watched her feet anyway.

The stone underfoot wasn’t steady in the way it used to be. It was steady like a tired person holding their face still so no one asked questions. Every few breaths the doubled hum ran up through her shoes, and the grit on the ground did a tiny lift-settle like it was answering something she couldn’t hear.

“Not folded,” someone argued. “Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth.”

“Entry was the ocean,” a man said. “My cousin’s out on the coast. If he’s alive, he’ll tell you—”

“You don’t know that,” another snapped. “You don’t know anything.”

“Sky’s still open,” someone murmured, and the people who heard it went quiet like they’d all looked up without wanting to.

“Stars in the day,” an old man said, and spat into the dust. “End-times.”

“Not end-times,” a brace captain barked nearby. “Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked.”

A baby cried. A child cried back. Different pitches, same need.

Chloen wanted to go find the older woman who’d wiped her face earlier and ask for the cloth again. The cloth had felt like a promise that skin could be clean.

She didn’t move.

If she moved, she might drift toward that gap. If she drifted toward the gap, someone might ask where her people were.

So she stayed still and practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One: a man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn’t want anyone to see it.
Two: a cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.
Three: rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.
Four: a pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.

The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the whole crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.

When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she’d been taught and hated that she’d been taught anything today.

She heard Tiffani’s voice before she saw her.

“Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped.”

Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.

Tiffani stood with her slate tucked under her arm again. She stood like the ground was an instrument and she didn’t trust it.

Jhace was near her, wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.

“Someone moved the ocean,” Jhace said.

Someone answered, low and flat. “Not possible.”

“It happened,” Jhace said.

Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and then they might see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.

Tiffani said, “We measure,” and Chloen didn’t know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn’t fix.

Jhace’s voice lowered. “And if the slate woman shows up again—”

Slate woman.

Chloen’s skin prickled. The woman with chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace’s hands.

Tiffani’s pencil tapped once.

Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones, even though it had fallen and broken and was done.

She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.

The bend in the terrace wall. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.

Her throat tightened.

She kept it to herself.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn’t bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people.

Chloen stared at the skyline until her eyes watered, then blinked hard and looked away.

“Okay,” she told herself again, quieter.

Then, because she didn’t know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn’t believe, but needed anyway.

“Still here.”

And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.

Not crying.
Not speaking.
Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the crowd noise thinned enough that words didn't get swallowed by it.

It happened in a pocket between tasks - after the meeting broke into work assignments, after the factor's people started pretending they were in charge again, after the brace captains peeled off to mark cracks and post rope lines.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm, not writing, just watching dust drift on the pressure pulses. Chloen hovered a step behind her.

Jhace came up on their left with a wedge bar still in his hand because setting it down felt like tempting fate.

He didn't soften his voice.

"Who are you?" he asked.

Chloen flinched. Tiffani shifted her weight to spare her knee without letting it look like a concession.

"Tiffani," she said. No title. No explanation.

"You live up here?"

"Above the high terrace. Glass-house."

Observatory, then. That explained the slate, the calm, the way she looked at the Rift like it was a problem that could be measured into submission.

Jhace looked down at the girl.

"And you."

Her chin lifted a fraction, stubborn. "Chloen."

"Chloen." He nodded once. "Good."

He meant: good you're alive. He didn't say the rest.

Tiffani's eyes stayed on the horizon. "You're Jhace," she said.

He looked at her.

"I heard it," she added.

"Yeah."

A pressure pulse rolled through the parapet stone. Dust lifted. Copper-gold grit along Tiffani's slate edge rose a hair and settled again.

Jhace felt the metallic hit at the back of his tongue. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

"There's a woman," he said. "Slate. Chalk. Counting."

Tiffani's pencil hand twitched at the word counting. Recognition.

"I saw her twice," Jhace went on. "Once after the slab. Once before the fire line. She wasn't looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands."

Chloen's gaze flicked between them. Kids noticed hunters.

"Describe her," Tiffani said.

"Dust cloak. Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it's keeping her upright."

Tiffani held that, then nodded once. "Not mine. Not from the glass-house."

"Do you know who she is?" Jhace asked.

"I know what she is," Tiffani said. "A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn't break."

Jhace stared past her shoulder at the worklines forming - hands passing stone, rope being tied, names being called.

Someone else was already trying to make a system out of what had happened.

"That's three of us," Jhace said, more to himself than them. "One who holds. One who measures. One who survives."

Chloen's mouth tightened. "I didn't -"

"I know," Jhace cut in. Not unkind. "You did."

Tiffani said, "The others will start asking."

"Let them," Jhace said.

His hands flexed once on the wedge bar. The tremor tried to rise. He forced it down by giving his fingers a job.

"But if that slate woman shows up again -" he started.

Tiffani's pencil tapped her slate once. A small, controlled marker.

" - you tell me," Jhace finished.

Chloen edged closer to the space between them, and Jhace didn't move away.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum sat under everything like a second heartbeat.

And for the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the bodies beside him.

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn't like looking up.

Looking up was how you saw what wasn't there anymore.

She stood on the wide stone flat where people passed rubble hand to hand and the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through cloth. Her sleeves were damp in places. Her skin didn't feel like it fit right.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself - terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs that pretended they were streets.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Corners that used to be straight leaned. A whole section of parapet was missing. Smoke rose from three places.

Chloen found the line where her street had been.

Not by a sign. Signs were gone or sideways.

By the bend in the terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.

Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One small window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa's pot hooks by the stove. A shelf with the chipped bowl they kept even though it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.

Chloen stared at the bend.

The tooth roofline was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.

Her mouth did the metallic thing again, like a coin pressed to the back of her tongue.

She swallowed because there was nowhere to put it.

That taste was getting old.

"Okay," she told herself. Quiet. Saying it loud would make it a lie people could hear.

She didn't cry.

Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn't decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet, and she didn't want to start and not be able to stop.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.

People talked around her like she wasn't there.

" - said the whole quarter folded," a woman whispered. "Just sank and set wrong."

"Not folded," someone argued back. "Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth."

"Entry was the ocean," a man said too loud. "My cousin's out on the coast. If he's alive, he'll tell you -"

"You don't know it was the ocean," another snapped. "You don't know anything."

"Sky's still open," someone murmured, and a few heads tilted without wanting to.

"Stars in the day," an old man said, and spat into the dust. "End-times."

"Not end-times," a brace captain barked nearby. "Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked."

A baby cried. A child cried back. A man called names into the air until his voice broke.

Chloen wanted to go find the older woman who'd wiped her face earlier and ask for the cloth again. The cloth felt like a promise that skin could be clean.

She didn't move.

If she moved, she might drift toward the gap. If she drifted toward the gap, someone might ask where her people were.

Where's your aunt, Chloen?

And if she answered, she'd have to say: probably under a roof that doesn't exist anymore.

So she stayed still and practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One: a man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn't want anyone to see it.
Two: a cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.
Three: rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit stone.

The inhale phase pulled at her lungs and the crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too.

When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she'd been taught and hated that she'd been taught anything today.

She heard Tiffani's voice before she saw her.

"Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped."

Chloen shifted her eyes sideways. Tiffani stood with her slate tucked under her arm. Jhace stood near her with a wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.

"Someone moved the ocean," Jhace said.

"Not possible," someone answered.

"It happened," Jhace said.

Tiffani said, "We measure."

Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.

She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.

The bend. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.

Her throat tightened.

She kept it to herself.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn't bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people.

"Okay," she told herself again.

Then, because she didn't know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing - one she didn't believe, but needed anyway.

"Still here."

And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.

Not crying.
Not speaking.
Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.
EOF

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the crowd noise thinned enough that words didn't get swallowed by it.

It found him between jobs - after the semicircle meeting broke into worklines, after the factor's people started repeating orders to people already moving, after the brace captains peeled off to post rope lines and chalk warnings.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm, not writing, just watching dust lift and settle with the pressure pulses. Chloen hovered a step behind her, close enough that a hand could grab cloth.

Jhace came up on their left with a wedge bar still in his hand. Putting it down felt like tempting fate.

"Who are you?" he asked.

Chloen flinched. Tiffani shifted her weight to spare her knee without making it a show.

"Tiffani," she said. Flat. Like a label on a box.

"You live up here?"

"Above the high terrace. Glass-house."

Observatory, then. That explained the slate, the way her eyes kept searching for a baseline that no longer existed.

Jhace looked down at the girl.

"And you?"

Her chin lifted a fraction, stubborn reflex. "Chloen."

"Chloen," he repeated, and the name stuck.

He nodded once. "Good." Not comfort. Inventory.

Tiffani's eyes stayed on the horizon. "You're Jhace," she said.

He looked at her.

"I heard it," she added.

"Yeah." His mouth went metallic again. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

He forced his attention back to the practical.

"There's a woman," he said. "Slate. Chalk. Counting."

Tiffani's pencil hand twitched at the word counting.

"I've seen her twice," Jhace went on. "Once after the slab. Once again before the fire line. She wasn't looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands."

Chloen's gaze flicked between them. Kids noticed watchers.

"Describe her," Tiffani said.

"Dust cloak. Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it's keeping her upright."

Tiffani held that for a beat, then shook her head once. "Not mine. Not from the glass-house."

"Do you know who she is?"

"I know what she is," Tiffani said. "A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn't break."

Jhace stared past them at the worklines: hands passing stone, names being called, rope going up. Someone else already building a system.

"That's three of us," Jhace said, more to himself than them. "One who holds. One who measures. One who survives."

Chloen's mouth tightened. "I didn't-"

"I know," Jhace cut in, not unkind. "You did."

Tiffani's gaze returned to the Rift without turning her head fully. "The others will start asking," she said.

"Let them," Jhace said.

The copper-gold grit along the edge of Tiffani's slate lifted a hair with the next pulse and settled again.

"If the slate woman shows up again," Jhace said, "you tell me."

Tiffani tapped her pencil once against the slate. A decision sound.

Chloen edged closer to the space between them. Jhace didn't move away.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum sat under everything like a second heartbeat.

For the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the bodies next to him.

---

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn't like looking up.

Looking up was how you saw what wasn't there anymore.

She stood on the wide stone flat where people kept passing rubble hand to hand, where voices had turned into lists and directions, where the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through a cloth. Her sleeves were still damp in places and her skin didn't feel like it fit right.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself - terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs that pretended they were streets. The city always looked like it had been built by someone who hated level ground.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Corners that used to be straight leaned. A whole section of parapet was missing. New scars ran where stone had slid and then stopped as if it had remembered rules mid-fall. Smoke rose from three places.

Chloen found the line where her street had been.

Not by a sign. Signs were gone or turned sideways.

By the bend in the terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.

Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One small window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa's pot hooks by the stove. A shelf with the chipped bowl they kept even though it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.

Chloen stared at the bend.

The tooth was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.

The metallic hit her again, like a coin pressed to the back of her tongue.

She swallowed it because there was nowhere to put it.

That taste was getting old.

"Okay," she told herself, but quiet. Saying it loud would make it a lie people could hear.

She didn't cry.

Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn't decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.

A man passed carrying a basket of broken brick. "Watch your feet, kid," he said without looking at her.

Chloen watched her feet.

The stone underfoot wasn't steady in the way it used to be. It was steady like a tired person holding their face still so nobody asked questions. Every few breaths the doubled hum ran up through her shoes, and the grit on the ground did a tiny lift-settle.

Grown-ups talked around her like she wasn't there.

"- said the whole quarter folded," a woman whispered.

"Not folded," someone argued back. "Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth."

"Entry was the ocean," a man said too loud. "My cousin's on the coast. If he's alive, he'll tell you."

"You don't know that," another snapped. "You don't know anything."

"Sky's still open," someone murmured, and a hush moved through the nearby line like a shadow.

"Stars in the day," an old man said, and spat. "End-times."

"Not end-times," a brace captain barked. "Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked."

Chloen listened because listening was how you survived grown-ups.

She didn't move.

If she moved, she might drift toward the gap. If she drifted toward the gap, someone might ask where her people were.

Where's your aunt, Chloen?

And if she answered, she would have to say: probably under a roof that doesn't exist anymore.

So she stood still and practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One: a man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn't want anyone to see it.

Two: a cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.

Three: rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.

Four: a pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.

The pull phase came and the crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.

When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she'd been taught and hated that she'd been taught anything today.

She heard Tiffani's voice before she saw her.

"Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped."

Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.

Tiffani stood near the parapet again, slate under her arm. Jhace was close, wedge bar still in hand, looking out like he was trying to see through stone.

"Someone moved the ocean," Jhace said.

Chloen's stomach went cold again even though she'd watched it happen.

"Not possible," someone answered.

"It happened," Jhace said.

Tiffani said, "We measure," and Chloen didn't know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn't fix.

Jhace's voice dropped. "And if the slate woman shows up again-"

Tiffani's pencil tapped once. Controlled.

Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones.

She looked up again, fast.

The bend. The missing tooth. The gap.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

She kept it to herself.

No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn't bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people.

Chloen blinked hard until her eyes stopped watering.

"Okay," she told herself again.

Then, because she didn't know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing - one she didn't believe, but needed anyway.

"Still here."

And she stayed on the stone flat where names were called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.

Not crying.

Not speaking.

Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.
EOF

# Chapter 8

Jhace waited until the crowd noise thinned enough that words didn't get swallowed by it.

It happened in a pocket between tasks - after the meeting broke into work assignments, after the factor's attendants started handing out blame with their orders, after the brace captains peeled off to mark cracks and tie rope lines.

Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm, not writing, just watching the pressure pulses push dust into slow drifts and then pull it back.

Chloen hovered a step behind her, small and steady, eyes tracking the ground like it might change its mind again.

Jhace came up on their left with a wedge bar still in his hand because putting it down felt like tempting fate.

"Who are you?" he asked.

Chloen flinched. Tiffani didn't.

"Tiffani," she said. No title. No explanation. Like a label on a jar.

"You live up here?"

"Above the high terrace. Glass-house." Observatory, then.

Jhace looked down at the girl. "And you."

Chloen lifted her chin a fraction. "Chloen."

"Chloen," he repeated, filing it into place. "Good." Not comfort. Confirmation.

Tiffani's eyes stayed on the horizon. "You're Jhace," she said.

He stared at her until she added, flat, "I heard it."

"Yeah." His mouth went metallic again. He swallowed. Stupid copper.

He shifted the wedge bar against his thigh to keep his hands busy. "There's a woman," he said. "Slate. Chalk. Counting."

Tiffani's pencil hand twitched once at the word counting.

"I saw her twice," Jhace continued. "Once after the slab. Once again before the fire line. She wasn't watching the Rift. She was watching my hands."

"Describe her," Tiffani said.

"Dust cloak. Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it's holding her up."

Tiffani nodded once. "Not mine. Not from the glass-house."

"Do you know who she is?"

"I know what she is," Tiffani said. "A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn't break."

Jhace looked past them to the worklines - hands passing stone, names being called, rope being tied. Someone else was already trying to make a system.

"If she shows up again," Jhace said, "you tell me."

Tiffani tapped her slate once. Controlled. Agreement.

Chloen edged closer to the space between them. Jhace didn't move away.

The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum sat under everything like a second heartbeat.

For the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the bodies next to him.

# Chapter 9

Chloen didn't like looking up.

Looking up was how you saw what wasn't there anymore.

She stood on the wide stone flat where people kept passing rubble hand to hand, where voices had turned into lists and directions, where the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through cloth.

Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself - terrace on terrace, doors above doors. The city always looked like it had been built by someone who hated level ground.

Now it looked like it had been bitten.

Chloen found the line where her street had been.

Not by a sign. Signs were gone or turned sideways.

By the bend in a terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.

Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa's pot hooks by the stove. A chipped bowl they kept even though it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.

Chloen stared at the bend.

The roofline tooth was missing.

There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.

The metallic hit her again. She swallowed because there was nowhere else to put it.

That taste was getting old.

"Okay," she told herself, but quiet, because saying it loud would make it a lie other people could hear.

She didn't cry. Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn't decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet.

Around her, grown-ups talked like she wasn't there.

"The whole quarter folded," a woman whispered. "Just sank and set wrong."

"Not folded," someone argued. "Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth."

"Entry was the ocean," a man said too loud. "My cousin's on the coast. If he's alive, he'll tell you."

"You don't know," another snapped. "You don't know anything."

"Sky's still open," an old man murmured, and the people who heard him went quiet like they'd all looked up without wanting to.

"Stars in the day," he added. "End-times."

A brace captain barked back, "Not end-times. Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked."

Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at the gap too long, someone would ask where her people were.

Where's your aunt, Chloen?

And if she answered, she would have to say: probably under a roof that doesn't exist anymore.

So she practiced being invisible.

She counted instead.

One: a man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn't want anyone to see it.
Two: a smashed cart turned into a supply table with cups lined in a neat row.
Three: rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.

The inhale phase came and the crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too. When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she'd been taught and hated that she'd been taught anything today.

She heard Tiffani's voice nearby - not loud, just clear.

"Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped."

Jhace answered someone, low. "Someone moved the ocean."

"Not possible," a man said.

"It happened," Jhace replied.

Chloen didn't look at them for long. Looking too long meant being seen.

She looked up once more anyway, fast, like touching a bruise.

The bend in the wall. The missing tooth. The gap.

Aunt Lessa most likely dead.

She kept it to herself. Saying it would not bring her back. Saying it would only make Chloen real to the wrong people, and today was already full of wrong.

"Still here," she whispered. Not a prayer. A check.

Then she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she did the only thing that still felt dependable.

Not crying.
Not speaking.
Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.
