## Chapter 11 — Tiffani (Cadence)

The bell sequence carried farther than the shouting.

Three measured strikes. Pause. Two fast. Pause. One long.

The sound repeated from station to station, each relay a fraction late. The doubled harmonic bent the metal tone. The cadence held anyway.

Tiffani looked up from a crack line she’d been watching. The seam in the paving opened a hair on each pulse and closed again on the slack phase. A rope had been tied across it. People stepped over the rope like it was an inconvenience, not a boundary.

She raised two fingers and pointed down.

“Do not step past the tie,” she said. “The seam is cycling.”

A man started to argue, then felt the next pulse under his feet and stopped. He backed up without speaking.

The bell sequence repeated.

Tiffani took her slate out and did not write. Not yet. A public notice wasn’t data. It was administration.

She moved toward the nearest central station on the upper terrace—an iron-post frame with an acoustics board bolted behind it and a notice rail where paper and slate sheets were clipped. The board had been rebuilt with scavenged timber. Its bolts were new. Someone had over-torqued two of them. She logged it mentally.

A runner stood under the board with a brass speaking cone and a throat wrap. His cloak carried grit in its seams.

He waited for the cadence to finish, then spoke.

“Notice from the Office of the Crown Administrator,” he said, reading off a slate in his own hands. “All able citizens and representatives of quarters to attend a live briefing at the Crown assembly ground. Address by Corlexi of Works. Subject: Planetary Structural Failure Event assessment and resource allocation directives. Attendance by quarter captains and trade leads required. Civil attendance permitted. Travel in escorted groups only. Departure windows posted by station.”

He did not say Manifestation.

He did not say the Rift.

He did not say the word throat.

He said Planetary Structural Failure Event.

A controlled label.

Tiffani watched the crowd response without recording it. People leaned toward the board to read the departure windows. Hands counted on fingers. A brace captain pushed through and tore down a torn sheet to replace it with a cleaner one.

Tiffani stepped close enough to read.

**Departure window: Upper Terrace — Third slack-phase after mid-bell. Escort assignment: Crown Guard intern squad.**

“Intern squad,” someone muttered behind her. “Children with spears.”

Tiffani didn’t comment. If the Crown had assigned interns, it meant the regular guard lines were either depleted or deployed elsewhere.

She turned.

Jhace was a few paces back, wedge bar resting against his thigh like an extra limb. His hands were steady when they held weight. When they didn’t, the tremor tried to surface and he kept his fingers moving on purpose.

Chloen stood near his elbow. Close. Not touching. The distance of someone who had learned proximity has rules.

Tiffani walked to them.

“We’re moving,” she said.

Jhace’s eyes flicked to the notice rail. “For what.”

“Briefing.”

“By who.”

Tiffani looked once toward the station runner. “Corlexi of Works.”

Jhace made a small sound through his nose. Not agreement. Not refusal. Assessment.

Chloen asked, “Do we have to?”

Tiffani held her slate against her ribs. “If we want to hear what is being said where it can be heard.”

A pulse ran through the station post. The board rattled. Loose bolts protested. The runner steadied the speaking cone with both hands.

Jhace’s jaw worked once. The metallic hit returned at the back of Tiffani’s tongue without invitation. She swallowed and didn’t change her face.

A new figure pushed through the cluster at the station: a young man in a Crown guard vest that didn’t fit clean at the shoulders. The vest straps were new. The boots weren’t. A short spear rode his back.

He stopped as if deciding where to put his body.

Then he smiled at no one in particular.

“Alright,” he said. “Group transport. We walk in a line. We pretend the line means something. We do not—”

He looked at the Rift in the distance and stopped mid-phrase as if the joke had snagged.

“—we do not,” he restarted, “fall into any holes in reality. If you see a hole in reality, point it out to the nearest adult. That would be me. Allegedly.”

He grimaced at his own last word. “Fuck. That sounded better in my head.”

Jhace’s mouth twitched once. He didn’t let it become a smile.

The guard intern glanced at Jhace’s wedge bar. “You planning to pry the sky back into place with that?”

Jhace didn’t answer.

The intern continued anyway, because silence didn’t stop him.

“I’m Bram,” he said. “Crown intern. Which means I am half trained, underpaid, and very likely to die of embarrassment before the planet finishes the job.”

He lifted a hand as if presenting himself like a bad tool.

Jhace held the silence for a beat.

Then, flat: “Finish it.”

Bram blinked. “Finish what.”

“The joke. You started a death sentence. You don’t get to drop it.”

Bram stared, then laughed once—short, surprised—and pointed at Jhace as if this was an argument he enjoyed losing.

“Right. Yes. Because if I don’t finish it, the universe wins,” Bram said. “Which is… not a real thing. That’s not—”

He paused, mouth half open. Reconsidered.

“Actually that might be the truest thing I’ve said today,” he muttered.

Tiffani moved one step closer to Jhace and Chloen, minimizing the space Bram had to perform into.

“Escort assignment,” she said. “Window is third slack-phase after mid-bell. We travel when posted.”

Bram squinted. “You talk like a notice rail.”

Tiffani did not look at him. “I talk like a schedule.”

“Even better,” Bram said. “Schedules don’t bleed.”

Jhace’s hand tightened on the wedge bar. The tremor tried to register. He forced it down by shifting grip.

Chloen made a small sound—almost a laugh—and then clamped her mouth closed as if she’d betrayed herself.

Bram saw it and brightened.

“See,” he said, pointing at Chloen. “Someone appreciates me. Or has a neurological malfunction.”

Jhace said, “Finish it.”

Bram exhaled hard. “Fine. The thing about schedules is they don’t bleed, but they do snap. Like…”

He looked at the station post, then at his own hands, then back at them.

“Like a belt on a forge wheel,” he said, and immediately winced. “No, that’s terrible. That’s your thing.”

Jhace’s eyes narrowed. “Keep going.”

Bram held up both hands, as if surrendering to the demand. “Like a tendon,” he tried. “No. Like a… like a—”

He stopped, stared at the sky wound again, and the humor drained to something practical.

“Look,” he said. “We walk. We watch the cracks. We breathe on the slack. We don’t run unless someone tells us to run.”

Tiffani noted the shift. He could read a room when he chose.

The bell cadence struck again—mid-bell—marking the central schedule.

Tiffani counted slack phases by ear and by knee.

One.

Two.

Three.

“Now,” she said.

Bram led them off the station flat and into the terrace corridor where the stone walls held sound in a hard line. He kept glancing back to see if the group stayed in the group.

Jhace walked at the rear. Chloen in the middle. Tiffani counted steps, watched shoulders, watched for limp timing.

Bram talked anyway.

“So,” he said, tone too light for the corridor. “You three look like a set of mismatched tools someone threw in a bag and hoped it would work. Hammer, lens, and… small knife?”

Chloen’s mouth twitched.

Tiffani ignored him.

Jhace said, “Finish it.”

Bram threw his head back against the stone for half a beat. “I hate you.”

“You don’t,” Jhace said.

Bram stared at him. “I don’t. That’s the problem.”

They turned a corner where the corridor widened. Ahead, other escorted groups flowed in the same direction: trade leads, brace captains, a few priests with throat wraps, two engineers hauling a ledger box between them.

Bram slowed to match the crowd speed and lowered his voice.

“Okay,” he said. “Question. You.” He pointed at Chloen without aiming the finger like a weapon. “Where’d you come from? Where’s your mom? Guardian? Someone who yells your name when stone falls?”

Chloen stopped walking for a fraction of a pulse.

Tiffani saw her shoulders lock, then release on the slack phase.

Jhace’s grip tightened.

Bram’s face shifted—too late—into recognition that he’d stepped on something he couldn’t see.

The corridor kept moving around them.

## Chapter 12 — Chloen (Answer)

Chloen kept walking because stopping made people look.

Bram’s question stayed in the air behind her like grit that wouldn’t settle.

Where’s your mom.

Guardian.

Someone who yells your name.

She could have said: dead.

She could have said: under a roof that isn’t a roof anymore.

She didn’t.

Her mouth did the metallic thing again—coin on the back of the tongue, then gone. She swallowed because it was what bodies did.

Jhace was behind her. She could feel it without turning. He walked like he was carrying weight even when his hands were empty.

Tiffani was beside her. Not touching. Close enough that Chloen could hear the pencil tap when Tiffani’s fingers adjusted on the slate.

Bram waited for an answer the way grown-ups waited when they thought they were being kind.

Chloen stared at the stone ahead. The corridor seams ran straight for six body-lengths, then shifted where the city had re-set wrong.

Her throat tightened.

She made herself say something that was true without being the whole truth.

“Don’t have one,” she said.

Bram’s mouth opened. A joke tried to come out. It died halfway.

“Oh,” he said.

Chloen kept her eyes forward. If she looked at his face, she would have to see the change in it.

She told herself, quiet: still here.

The crowd around them pressed closer as the corridor narrowed again. Someone carrying a crate bumped Chloen’s shoulder and kept going without apology.

Chloen rubbed the spot like it mattered. Small pain. Simple.

Bram said, softer, “Alright. Okay.”

He cleared his throat like that was a reset.

“I didn’t mean—”

He stopped. Changed direction mid-sentence.

“I mean I did mean. I asked. That’s on me.”

Jhace’s voice came from behind her, flat and immediate. “Stop digging.”

Bram exhaled once. “Yeah.”

Chloen felt a laugh try to rise at the word digging and the timing of it and hated herself for it.

She didn’t let it out.

They reached a stair run where the terrace corridor opened onto a sloped ramp with guard rails bolted into stone. The rails had been re-seated with fresh wedges. A brace captain stood at the top, counting bodies through.

Chloen watched hands instead of faces.

Hands told the truth.

Bram’s hands were open. Empty. Not reaching for her.

Jhace’s hands were tight on his wedge bar. Tiffani’s hands held slate and pencil like tools.

Chloen kept walking.

Her home was a gap.

Her aunt was most likely dead.

She kept it in her chest where it wouldn’t get written down.

---

Bram had walked escorts before.

Not official ones. Not Crown ones.

Back when he ran errands for men who smiled too much, escort meant guiding someone through a market so they didn’t get robbed by the wrong crew. You did it with jokes. You did it with posture. You did it by making yourself the most distracting thing in the street.

This was different.

The crowd moved like a machine missing a gear. Not chaotic. Just irregular. Each pulse of the doubled hum changed how bodies timed their breath and their feet.

Bram kept his pace on the slack because the girl did.

He’d watched her freeze when he asked. He’d watched her make the answer small.

Don’t have one.

That meant: don’t ask again.

He did not ask again.

He tried to find a joke anyway because jokes were how he kept panic from getting teeth.

The first one rose and died in his mouth.

The second was worse.

He swallowed both.

“Alright,” he said instead, voice low. “Ramp’s slick. Watch the seam at the third rail post. It shifts.”

Tiffani’s head turned a fraction toward him, as if marking that he’d said something useful.

Jhace grunted once. Agreement by minimal sound.

Chloen didn’t look back.

The assembly ground came into view beyond the ramp: a broad bowl cut into the upper terrace, old stone seating rebuilt with timber scaffolds and sound boards. An amplifying cone of riveted metal rose at the center like an inverted bell, pointed toward the stands.

Bram’s squad lead waved them toward an intake lane.

“Escort group nine,” Bram called, reading the tag on his own vest like it might make him legitimate. “Three civilians. One brace-adjacent.”

Jhace gave him a look.

Bram added, quickly, “Fine. Two civilians and a man made of rebar.”

Jhace said, “Finish it.”

Bram’s smile came out even though he didn’t want it to. “Rebar holds. Until it doesn’t.”

Chloen made the smallest sound—half laugh, half breath.

They reached the lane split where guards directed people to seating blocks.

Bram pointed. “That section. Third row. Stay inside the rope. If you fall, fall inward. I’m not catching anyone. I’m an intern.”

Tiffani moved first. Jhace followed. Chloen came last, stepping where they stepped.

As they crossed the rope line, the group’s spacing changed. Not by command. By circumstance. Bodies absorbed into bodies.

Bram stood at the lane mouth and watched them go.

Tiffani’s slate edge caught light and then disappeared behind shoulders.

Jhace’s wedge bar vanished in the crowd like it had never existed.

Chloen’s dust-matted head was visible for three steps and then wasn’t.

Bram’s throat tightened.

He didn’t like it.

He told himself it was nothing.

He told himself it was better.

He told himself the joke had been stupid and he deserved the look she hadn’t given him.

His hands stayed open at his sides.

He didn’t follow.

He was assigned to the lane.

He watched the crowd swallow a child and felt the first real weight of the day settle where humor usually lived.

## Chapter 13 — Camera (Assembly Ground)

Rope lines split the bowl into lanes.

Guards in Crown vests and borrowed boots directed bodies by hand signals. Brace captains marked cracked steps with red chalk. Trade leads held up tokens to prove quarter assignment.

Sound boards had been bolted to the old stone seating in angled tiers. Rivets shone fresh where they’d been replaced. A central speaking platform sat below, backed by an amplifying cone of riveted metal and timber bracing.

Clerks set up tables under canvas awnings at the bowl’s edges. Slates stacked in rows. Ink pots. Wax seals. A scale for weighing metal tokens.

A banner hung over the main intake: the Crown crest on stitched cloth, reinforced with thin copper wire that vibrated on each harmonic pulse.

Four other emblems appeared on armbands and collar pins throughout the crowd.

A square-threaded knot in black cord.

A drop-shaped plate of pale metal.

A loop of linked rings.

A fractured bar of iron.

Priests and lay adherents wore them without uniform agreement. Some hid them under wraps. Some displayed them at the throat.

The Crown officers held the highest rows closest to the speaking cone. City factors gathered beside them, rings flashing when hands moved. Pipe guild engineers held a block near the left, ledgers on knees. Forge and brace crews filled the lower tiers where their bodies could move fast if stone shifted.

Messengers moved between blocks carrying clipped notice sheets.

A new table had been set beside the clerks’ awning: a registry board with empty columns and a stack of numbered slates.

The column headers were written in clean block script.

**NAME. QUARTER. TRADE. OBSERVED ANOMALY. ASSIGNMENT.**

A second board leaned against the first, its header covered with cloth.

Crowds settled in uneven waves. The doubled hum ran under the bowl, lifting dust and letting it fall again. People adjusted their breathing without instruction.

At the platform, a man in a clean coat checked the speaking cone bolts with his fingertips and then stepped back into position.

Bells at the rim struck the notice cadence once more.

Three measured strikes. Pause. Two fast. Pause. One long.

The sound boards held it and threw it back.

The assembly ground quieted by degrees.

## Chapter 11 — Tiffani (Cadence)

**Camera:** Bell towers on the upper terraces. Hammered bronze mouths. Ropes vibrating. Dust lifting from parapet seams on the slack phase, settling on the pull.

Three measured strikes. Pause. Two fast. Pause. One long.

The cadence relayed down the city in delayed layers. Lower stations answered as the sequence reached them, each repeat a fraction late. The doubled harmonic bent the metal tone. The pattern held anyway.

**Sensory intrusion:** Her knee registered the third strike as load.

**Tiffani POV:** She stopped mid-step and listened for the repeat. Confirmed. Central-station notice.

She did not like bell protocols. Metal-based systems drifted under the harmonic. Cadences corrupted. False alarms overlapped. She had already watched three sectors ring evacuation for a crack that never opened.

This cadence was not evacuation.

She moved toward the nearest notice station because paper did not ring wrong. Not yet.

At the board, a clerk had already pinned a stamped sheet under a brass bar. The wax seal was fresh. The emblem impressed into it was a square—Form.

Tiffani read the header first. Titles mattered because they signaled which lies were legally permitted.

**OFFICE OF INFRASTRUCTURE AND PUBLIC WORKS**

**NOTICE OF BRIEFING AND SEMINAR**

**Administrator Corlexi**

**Assembly required for quarter representatives and escort leads.**

Below, the text was dense and intentionally non-specific. It named a classification—Planetary Structural Failure Event—then listed procedures.

Report to West Riser amphitheater at mid-slack.

Bring local measurements.

Do not attempt replication of observed anomalies.

Submit witness accounts through station captains.

No mention of “powers.” No mention of “gift.” No mention of “manifestation.” The word anomaly appeared three times, each time attached to structure or load.

Tiffani read it again, slower, looking for what was missing.

There was no statement of cause.

There was no statement of scale.

There was no promise.

It was a logistics document pretending to be reassurance.

She took her slate out, wrote one line, and underlined it.

**Briefing: Corlexi. Form emblem. Controlled narrative.**

Her pencil paused.

If Corlexi was calling for “quarter representatives,” then the Crown was already building a map of who could speak for which survivors. That implied either coordination or containment.

Both required attendance.

She turned from the board and scanned the flat.

Jhace was visible by motion before he was visible by face: wedge bar in hand, shoulders moving through a load pattern like he belonged to the stone. He was leveraging a broken lintel while two others dragged debris clear. His hands shook when he was idle. When he worked, the tremor reduced.

Chloen stayed within three paces of him without touching. Not hovering. Tracking. A child practicing the radius of safety.

Tiffani walked to them on the slack phase.

“Notice,” she said.

Jhace didn’t look up. “Bell said so.”

He said it as if the bell was an instruction, not an alarm.

Tiffani held the slate so he could see the stamp and the header without reading the body. Jhace’s eyes moved once across the top line.

“Corlexi,” he said.

“You know the name,” Tiffani noted.

“Everyone knows the name,” Jhace said. “Works office. Brace funding. Pipe permits.”

Chloen leaned in a fraction, eyes on the square emblem. She didn’t ask questions. She watched adults decide.

Tiffani pointed her pencil at the location line. “West Riser. Mid-slack. Attendance requested for quarter representatives.”

Jhace’s jaw worked. “Requested,” he repeated.

Tiffani did not correct him. The Crown rarely requested.

A station marshal moved through the workline calling for “escort leads” and “order runners.” His voice cracked from repetition. Behind him, two Crown guards in fresh tabards set a rope corridor, guiding people uphill.

The marshal saw Tiffani’s slate and redirected. “You,” he said. “Glass-house?”

“Instrument station,” Tiffani said.

He nodded once, satisfied by the existence of a label. “You’re going to the briefing.”

“Yes.”

He pointed behind him. “Escort assigned.”

A young man stepped forward, helmet under his arm, cloak tied wrong at the throat as if he’d dressed in a hurry and resented the fabric. The Crown crest on his tabard looked new enough to still believe in itself.

He smiled like it was a weapon.

“Bram Tallow,” he said, then paused, as if realizing he’d offered his name too fast. “Interning. Guard track. Today I’m apparently a shepherd, which is fine, except I don’t have a staff and you lot look like you bite.”

Tiffani stared at him.

Jhace stared harder.

Chloen’s mouth twitched.

Bram continued anyway because silence was unbearable to him. “If it helps, I also bite. It’s a community thing.” He tilted his head, reconsidered. “That sounded—worse than I meant. You know what I mean. Teeth. Everyone’s got ’em.”

Jhace said, “Finish the thought.”

Bram blinked. “What?”

“You started it,” Jhace said. “Finish it.”

Bram’s smile widened, trapped in its own momentum. “Right. Fine. Everyone bites because everyone’s hungry because the world ate our lunch, chewed it, and spit it back out in powdered metal.”

Tiffani’s pencil lifted, then stopped. She did not write that.

Chloen made a small sound. Not laughter, exactly. Air trying to escape without permission.

Bram noticed and pointed at her like it mattered. “See? The small one gets it.”

Tiffani said, “Escort parameters.”

Bram’s face shifted, offended by competence. “Parameters. Sure. We’re going from this station up two terraces and across the West Riser span to the amphitheater. Rope corridors are active. Don’t cross chalk. Don’t get yourself crushed. If you fall, try to fall where it’s inconvenient for the Crown so they’ll notice.”

Jhace said, “You’re a guard.”

“I’m an intern guard,” Bram corrected. “There’s a difference. Real guards get to pretend they know what they’re doing.” He opened his mouth, caught himself, and tried again. “I mean—also I don’t know what I’m doing.”

Jhace nodded once as if that was finally honest.

Tiffani turned. “We move on slack phase. The pull will increase near the open faces. Keep to stone ribs.”

Bram’s eyes flicked to her knee and away, as if he’d noticed the hitch and decided not to be kind about it. “Copy. Slack phase. We all breathe like we’re courting the air.”

Tiffani didn’t respond.

They joined the rope corridor with a dozen others. The doubled hum ran under the paving like a second instruction set, and the crowd moved in involuntary synchronization: inhale shallow on pull, fill on slack.

Bram walked ahead, talking at the air.

“West Riser used to be where the Crown did parades,” he said. “Now it’s where they do panic with better signage. Progress.” He started to laugh, stopped, and tried a different angle. “Look, if we make it there, we get to hear a man explain the end of the world with the enthusiasm of a ledger. You’ll love it. Especially you.” He nodded at Tiffani.

Tiffani said, “Do not address me as an audience.”

Bram grinned. “She hates it. Perfect.”

Jhace said, “Keep walking.”

Bram kept walking. “I am walking. I’m walking so hard I’m practically running.” He opened his mouth again, reconsidered the metaphor mid-formation. “That also sounds like—never mind.”

Jhace said, “Finish it.”

Bram groaned. “Running. I’m practically running. Like a valve trying to hold pressure and failing politely.”

Chloen’s shoulders shook once. She covered it by adjusting her grip on the rope.

Tiffani marked in her mind: **Escort uses humor as tension vent. Variable noise. Persistent.**

At the next terrace turn, Bram glanced back at Chloen, softened for a fraction, and then ruined it by speaking.

“So,” he said, too casual. “Where’d you come from, kid? Where’s your mom? Your guardian?”

Chloen went still.

Tiffani heard the silence before she saw it: a gap in the group’s rhythm.

Jhace’s head turned, slow.

Bram’s smile faltered, caught on the edge of his own question.

The bell sequence echoed again in the distance, steady and indifferent.

## Chapter 12 — Chloen (Answer)

Chloen didn’t like questions that made people look at her.

She liked being small. Small meant you could slide into gaps. Small meant you could disappear between elbows when stone decided to fall.

Bram’s question made a space around her.

Where’s your mom.

Where’s your guardian.

Her chest tightened like the parapet edge had come back and sat on her ribs.

She saw Aunt Lessa’s pot hooks.

She saw the chipped bowl.

She saw the missing tooth roofline.

She did not look up the mountain face. Looking up was how you found the gap.

Her mouth did the metal thing again. Not a taste exactly. A hit. Like licking a coin.

She swallowed and told herself, **don’t cry. don’t cry.**

Bram was still smiling, but now it looked stuck. Like he’d walked into a door he didn’t know was there.

Jhace didn’t say anything. That made it worse.

Tiffani didn’t say anything either. That was normal.

Chloen kept her eyes on Bram’s boots. Mud on one toe. Ash on the other. He’d stepped in two kinds of mess.

“My aunt,” she said.

The word came out flat. She hated it. It sounded like a request.

Bram’s smile tried to recover. “Cool. Aunts are—” He stopped. Restarted. “Aunts are like… second parents. Like—”

Jhace said, “Finish it.”

Bram made a pained face. “Like a spare clamp you don’t appreciate until the main one breaks.”

Chloen huffed. She couldn’t help it.

It wasn’t funny.

It was stupid.

It was also the only thing in the last day that wasn’t trying to kill her.

“She’s not here,” Chloen added, quick, before anyone asked where. “I don’t know.”

That was true enough to not feel like lying.

Bram’s voice dropped. “Yeah. Okay.”

He looked away first. That helped.

The rope corridor carried them forward. People moved around them in strings: families with hands locked, strangers with nothing to hold but their own sleeves, injured people half-supported by anyone who still had strength.

Chloen listened to grown-up talk like she always did.

“Corlexi’s going to tell us what happened,” someone said behind them.

“He’ll tell you what the Crown wants you to believe,” someone else replied.

“Doesn’t matter,” a third voice cut in. “I want bread.”

Chloen wanted bread too. She wanted her aunt. Bread was easier.

The doubled hum ran up through her soles. Two notes. One old. One new. She timed her breathing like Tiffani had said: shallow on pull, fill on slack.

Chloen tried not to think about the ocean.

She tried not to think about the Rift.

She tried not to think about the slate woman with chalk.

At a stair landing, Bram slowed so a limping couple could pass. He kept his body angled between them and the crowd, like a guard was supposed to. He was better at that than he was at questions.

Chloen watched him without meaning to.

He smelled like sweat and lamp oil and the faint sour edge of fear that everyone had now. He tried to cover it with jokes the way people covered cracks with plaster.

It didn’t work.

But it was something.

They crossed the West Riser span. The stone ribs opened to a view of the amphitheater cut into the terrace face—wide benches, a central floor, reflector horns bolted to the back wall to throw sound up and out.

Chloen had seen it before from a distance. Parades. Speeches. Crown announcements that meant nothing to children.

Now it looked like a bowl waiting to be filled with bodies.

Rope lines fed into entrances. Guards directed streams. Clerks at tables stamped wrists with ink marks that looked like bruises.

Chloen didn’t want to be stamped.

She stayed close to Jhace’s wedge bar because it looked like a weapon.

At the amphitheater gate, Bram stepped aside to let a larger group funnel in. He gestured them forward with a hand that tried not to shake.

Jhace moved first. Tiffani followed, slate already in her hand. Chloen hesitated one breath.

Bram looked down at her.

He didn’t make a joke.

That was worse than the jokes.

Chloen stepped past him.

She didn’t look back.

### Bram

Bram kept his face neutral until the three of them were inside.

Neutral felt wrong. Like wearing someone else’s clothes.

He watched Chloen’s head disappear into the crowd—dust-matted hair, shoulders too tight, steps too careful like the ground was a liar.

He’d asked a normal question.

Normal before the sky tore.

Normal before kids had to learn how to breathe on slack phase.

Now it was a knife.

He told himself he couldn’t have known.

He told himself that was bullshit.

He shifted his weight, felt the hum in the stone, and tried to build a joke out of it because that was what he did.

Nothing landed.

He watched Jhace’s broad back cut through bodies like a brace beam. Watched Tiffani’s slate angle toward everything that hurt. Watched Chloen drift in their wake like she’d decided they were the safest moving objects in a collapsing city.

Bram’s throat tightened.

He hated that.

He hated that he cared.

He hated that it made him quieter.

A senior guard—real tabard, real crest, no wrong tie at the throat—passed behind him and said, “Keep the line moving, intern.”

Bram forced the grin back onto his face because authority demanded performance.

“Always moving,” he said. “We’re a river. A beautiful, terrified river.” He winced at his own metaphor and tried to fix it mid-sentence. “A river with paperwork.”

The senior guard didn’t laugh.

Bram looked at the gate again.

Inside, Chloen had already been swallowed by benches and bodies.

He couldn’t fix the question.

He couldn’t un-ask it.

All he could do was keep other people from getting trampled on the way to the man with the square emblem.

He turned back to the rope corridor, lifted his voice, and became a guard again.

“Keep to the right,” he called. “If you fall, fall inward. Outward is where the stone gets ideas.” He started to add something about ideas and stone having brains, then stopped himself. Too stupid. Not the right day.

He let the silence sit for one breath.

Then he said, softer, mostly to himself, “Sorry, kid.”

And kept the line moving.

## Chapter 13 — Camera (West Riser)

The amphitheater filled by flow, not announcement.

Rope corridors fed the entrances in steady strings. Guards in Crown tabards adjusted knots and moved bodies left or right with flat hands. Clerks at tables stamped wrists with ink marks—triangles for medical, circles for water allocation, a single line for counted-and-cleared.

Sound hardware ringed the back wall: reflector horns bolted into stone ribs, copper-mesh throats aimed over the benches. Apprentices climbed ladders to tighten braces, tapping bolts, listening for chatter under the doubled hum.

A central floor had been cleared. Chalk lines divided it into lanes: officials, brace captains, guild leads, clergy.

Four emblems repeated in small places—on collars, on sashes, on wax seals.

Square.

Drop.

Loop.

Fracture.

They did not sit together.

Crown factors held the front benches: clean coats dusted at the hem, rings on both hands, ledger clerks at their knees. Brace captains stood at aisle ends, knuckles split, chalk and rope at their belts. Pipe-guild engineers clustered in a knot to the left, sleeves rolled, hands dark with grit, arguing in low voices over valve failures and pressure reversals.

Glass-house observers arrived in pairs or alone, carrying slates protected under coats. They did not wear the Crown crest.

Purity clergy moved along the side aisles, stopping to touch shoulders, to speak in close tones, to direct the newly injured toward the medical lane.

Form administrators walked the central floor in straight lines, measuring crowd density by eye. They repositioned rope posts by inches to change flow. They did not look up at the Rift.

Wholeness adherents gathered near the back where sound was strongest, palms open on the stone ribs as if feeling for pattern. Entropy crews—labor gangs with patched coats and no emblem—hovered at the edges, waiting to see which resources would be locked behind which lists.

A registry table was set at the right wall. It was larger than the ration tables. Blank forms stacked in neat piles. Ink pots weighted with scrap metal. A stamp block with new carvings.

Names began to accumulate.

The Crown guard track stationed interns at the outer gates. Older guards watched them like equipment that might fail.

At the central floor, a raised platform held a speaking stand braced to the stone. Behind it, two horn operators tested the reflector alignment with short bursts of sound—clapped syllables, a tapped bar, a shouted count. The echoes returned clean enough to be useful.

On the slack phase, the crowd inhaled.

On the pull, the crowd leaned.

The rhythm taught itself.

A man in a clean coat with a square emblem at the collar stepped onto the platform. He did not gesture for attention.

The bell cadence outside repeated once more.

Three measured strikes. Pause. Two fast. Pause. One long.

The amphitheater quieted by obedience, not trust.

## Chapter 11 — Tiffani (Cadence)

**Camera:** Bell towers on the upper terraces. Hammered bronze mouths. Ropes vibrating. Dust lifting from parapet seams on the slack phase, settling on the pull.

Three measured strikes. Pause. Two fast. Pause. One long.

The cadence relayed down the city in delayed layers. Lower stations answered as the sequence reached them, each repeat a fraction late. The doubled harmonic bent the tone. The sequence held.

**Sensory intrusion:** Tiffani’s knee registered each bell strike as a clean impulse through stone. She adjusted weight to the slack phase without naming it comfort.

**Tiffani POV:** Three–two–one meant central-station notice. Not evacuation. Not collapse alarm. Not fire. A directive.

She closed her slate and stood up from the crack line she’d been watching. The seam in the paving had been opening a hair on each pulse and closing again. It was stable within current tolerance. She had chalked it with two parallel marks and a diagonal: monitor, do not load.

The bell cadence repeated.

People on the flat turned their heads at the same time. The motion was mechanical. Training. Habit. In a city built out of terraces, habit mattered more than courage.

A brace captain nearest the rope line lifted two fingers. “Station,” he called. “Cadence means a posting. Keep moving. No crowding the lip.”

The worklines did not stop. Stone still passed hand to hand. Rope stayed tight across the cracked section. But eyes drifted toward the nearest stair throat.

Tiffani moved with them.

The central station on this terrace sat in a widened bay where two stairwells met and the paving was thickened with iron braces. A board of slate panels stood against the wall, each panel rubbed clean and re-chalked with the day’s directives.

A courier was there already, breath rough, cloak dusted. He held a stamped strip of metal at chest height while a station clerk copied its contents onto the slate board.

The strip carried the Crown’s seal: a square within a square. Form.

Tiffani stepped close enough to read without pushing.

The posting did not use poetic language.

It used infrastructure terms.

**NOTICE — CENTRAL ASSEMBLY / PUBLIC BRIEFING**

**Issued by:** Office of the Chief Administrator of Infrastructure and Public Works

**Administrator:** Corlexi (Form)

**Subject:** Planetary Structural Failure Event (PSFE) — Initial 48-Hour Assessment

**Directive:** Assembly of station captains, brace leadership, pipe quarter leads, Glass-House observers, and citizen delegates.

**Location:** West Riser Amphitheater (upper basin)

**Time:** Two bell cycles from posting.

**Purpose:** Dissemination of verified observations. Standardization of reporting protocols. Resource allocation directives.

It did not mention “manifestation.”

It did not mention “Soulpulse.”

It did not mention people holding stone in midair or water changing direction as if it had been given an instruction.

It did include “standardization of reporting.”

Tiffani’s pencil moved before she decided.

She opened her slate and wrote one line.

**Corlexi: briefing. PSFE terminology adopted. Reporting protocols pending.**

Then, smaller:

**Omissions notable.**

A station guard leaned in and read the posting as if he could pull safety out of it by force. “So… we go,” he said.

A woman behind him muttered, “We go and we get told what the Crown wants us to think.”

The guard flinched. He didn’t turn. No one wanted a confrontation beside a posting board.

Tiffani stepped back. The cadence rang again, confirming the time window.

She needed position. She needed audio clarity. She needed to see how Corlexi framed the known variables.

She scanned the station bay and found Jhace by process of elimination. He stood slightly off the densest cluster, wedge bar still in his hand like an extension of his arm. Dust on his sleeves. Eyes moving over the board and then over the crowd as if he was checking for load shifts.

Chloen was near him, half behind his shoulder, looking at the bell rope as if the rope might decide to fall.

Tiffani approached.

Jhace did not greet her. He didn’t greet anyone. He said, “Posting?”

“Yes,” she said. “Briefing. West Riser Amphitheater. Two bell cycles.”

Jhace’s jaw worked once. The metallic hit him at the back of the tongue. He swallowed it without comment.

Chloen asked, small, “Do we have to go?”

Tiffani did not answer with reassurance.

“It is a controlled point,” she said. “Better than rumor.”

Jhace glanced down the stairs toward the city’s lower terraces. He counted something silent. Then he nodded once.

“Short distance,” he said, as if that made it tolerable.

They moved.

The route to West Riser ran along a terrace spine where the paving was broad and the parapets intact. Rope lines marked unstable edges. Brace captains stood at intervals with chalk and short poles, tapping stone and listening.

Crowds moved uphill in loose clusters, following the bell cadence like water follows grade.

At the next station throat, a Crown guard detail had formed a funnel. Not to block. To separate.

A clerk sat at a table with a ledger. A second clerk held a bundle of stamped tokens. Guards directed people into lanes.

**Camera:** Rope stanchions. Chalk arrows on stone. A horn-shaped sound reflector mounted above the amphitheater approach, angled toward the basin like a metal ear.

Tiffani noted the reflector first. The Crown had anticipated crowd density and used acoustics. Form.

A young guard in a half-polished breastplate stepped into their lane and held up a hand.

He was not old enough to have a permanent line in his face. His uniform smelled of oil and new leather. His helmet was clipped to his belt like he wanted his head seen.

“In-coming group,” he announced, then paused as if he’d forgotten the next word. “Escort assignment.”

He checked a slate tag on his wrist.

“Bram Tallow,” he said, then immediately added, “Interning. With the Crown. Which—” he shifted his weight and looked at the rubble behind them as if seeking a better metaphor “—is like being the smallest rivet in the biggest, most expensive boiler. You don’t get to pick the heat.”

He stopped mid-thought.

“Gods, that’s stupid,” he muttered, but not quietly.

Jhace’s eyes narrowed. “Finish it.”

Bram blinked. “What?”

“You started it. Finish it,” Jhace said, voice flat.

Bram’s mouth twisted. “Fine. If the boiler blows, the rivet gets turned into… a moral lesson. Or shrapnel.” He frowned. “That’s worse. That’s not even funny.”

Jhace didn’t let it drop. “Keep going.”

Tiffani watched the exchange as interference. Humor introduced noise in a directive corridor.

“Bram,” she said, “confirm: you are assigned to escort our group to West Riser Amphitheater.”

He snapped his attention to her, relief visible as compliance.

“Yes. Assigned. Escort. I’m the rope line with a mouth.” He started to grin, then hesitated. “That’s also stupid. Forget it.”

Jhace’s gaze pinned him. “Don’t.”

“Don’t what?” Bram asked.

“Don’t try to back out,” Jhace said. “You dug the hole. Lie in it.”

Chloen made a small sound that might have been a laugh if she’d trusted the day enough to allow it. She covered it by rubbing her nose.

Bram caught it. His grin came back, quick. “See? The kid gets it.”

“The kid does not need it,” Tiffani said.

Bram’s eyebrows went up. “She needs something. Everyone needs something. You two look like you’re chewing nails for breakfast.”

Jhace’s hand tightened on the wedge bar. “I’m not chewing anything.”

Bram started, “That’s a shame because—” then stopped, rethought, and grimaced. “No. That goes somewhere gross. I’m not saying it.”

Jhace’s eyes narrowed again. “Finish it.”

Tiffani exhaled through her nose, once. Not a hum. A controlled vent.

Bram looked between them, offended and amused at the same time. “You’re a menace,” he told Jhace.

Jhace didn’t deny it.

Tiffani stepped forward. “Movement,” she said. “We will miss the first portion.”

Bram pivoted into escort posture immediately, as if glad to have a clear task. He gestured down the corridor with two fingers. “This way. Stay inside the rope. Don’t lean on the parapet. If you fall, I have to do paperwork, and my handwriting looks like a drunk spider.”

Chloen’s mouth twitched.

Jhace said, “Finish the spider.”

Bram walked. “The spider falls in an inkpot. Then the Crown promotes it.”

Jhace nodded, satisfied in a way that made no sense to Tiffani.

They moved along the terrace spine.

The doubled harmonic stayed underfoot. Two layers of vibration. One old baseline, one new. Tiffani adjusted her stride to the slack phase. She watched Jhace do the same without acknowledging it.

Bram noticed the breath timing in the crowd and decided it was an audience.

“So,” he said, “we’re all breathing like fish in a barrel. Except the barrel is the planet, and—” he stopped again. “No. That’s also stupid.”

Jhace said, “Finish it.”

Tiffani hated it.

Chloen chuckled anyway, small and involuntary.

Bram shot her a look like he’d been handed a medal. “Thank you.”

Chloen looked at the ground, embarrassed by her own sound.

They passed a registry table. A clerk held out a token to a man and asked his name, station, family count. The man answered like the questions were a weight.

Tiffani noted the token shape: square cutout, Form seal. Standardization in progress.

Bram kept walking until the path widened and the amphitheater approach opened into view.

He slowed, then glanced back over his shoulder at Chloen.

His voice shifted slightly—still joking, but reaching for something that would land safer.

“Hey,” he said. “Where’d you come from, anyway? Who’s your—”

He searched for the word and found the wrong one.

“—your mom? Your guardian?”

Chloen stopped.

Tiffani felt the crowd noise tighten. A minor tension event. No stone movement. Human movement.

Jhace’s grip on the wedge bar changed.

Bram realized, half a breath late, that he’d stepped on a crack he hadn’t marked.

## Chapter 12 — Chloen / Bram (The Question)

Chloen had been doing fine.

Fine meant: feet moving. Eyes down. Breathe on the slack. Don’t look at the gap where things used to be.

Fine meant: laugh only when it leaked out and then swallow it back down.

Then the guard with the too-loud mouth asked the wrong thing.

Where’d you come from. Where’s your mom. Your guardian.

Chloen’s stomach dropped like she’d missed a stair.

She didn’t have a mom here.

She had an aunt.

Aunt Lessa.

Aunt Lessa was under a roof that wasn’t a roof anymore.

Most likely.

Chloen kept that sentence behind her teeth because if it got out, it would become a thing other people could touch.

She stared at Bram’s boots. They were new. The leather still held shape. The buckles didn’t have rust.

He didn’t know.

Of course he didn’t know.

People asked questions like that because they still believed the world had answers.

Chloen swallowed. Metallic. Again. Not coin, not blood. Just that taste that showed up when she didn’t want anything else in her mouth.

That taste was getting old.

She could lie.

She could say a name and let it float.

But then someone might go looking.

And they might come back with the wrong kind of pity.

She looked past Bram’s knee at the rope line and the amphitheater approach beyond. People were still moving. No one had time to stop for long.

Jhace stood to her left, wedge bar angled down like a threat that was also a tool. He didn’t look at her face. He looked at Bram.

Tiffani stood to her right, slate tucked under her arm. Her eyes were on Chloen’s mouth like she was tracking a variable.

Chloen hated that.

She liked that Tiffani didn’t try to comfort.

Comfort made you visible.

Chloen said, “Don’t have one.”

The words came out flat.

Bram blinked.

Chloen added, because silence felt like danger, “Just me.”

The sentence was true enough to not feel like a lie.

Bram’s grin faltered. It didn’t vanish. It shifted, like a joke trying to find a safer place to land.

“Oh,” he said.

He tried again, softer. “Well. That’s—”

He stopped. Rethought. His face tightened like he’d bitten something sour.

“That’s… shit,” he finished, and the swear sounded honest because it wasn’t dressed up.

Chloen didn’t answer.

She looked at the amphitheater instead. Big stone bowl. Metal horn reflectors mounted around the rim. Rope stanchions guiding people into lanes.

“Move,” Jhace said.

Not harsh. Not kind. Just directive.

Chloen moved.

She walked inside the rope line and kept her eyes on the backs of people’s coats.

She did not look back.

She did not say Aunt Lessa.

She kept it to herself.

Halfway to the entry gates, she heard Bram behind them.

His voice was quieter now.

“Sorry,” he said, and then, because he couldn’t stop himself, “I mean— not sorry like… I’m not apologizing, I just— gods, that sounded worse.”

Jhace said, without turning, “Finish it.”

Chloen heard Bram huff a laugh that didn’t want to be one.

“Fine,” Bram said. “I stepped in it. I’m… stepping out. That’s the whole thing. I’m done.”

Chloen’s shoulders loosened a fraction.

She hated that she cared.

The amphitheater gates opened into a corridor where clerks and guards directed people by token color and station mark. Groups broke apart. Families pulled toward one another by name.

Chloen saw a line of children being guided to a low wall under supervision of two older women with water cups.

Safety by clustering.

She drifted toward it without deciding.

She heard Jhace and Tiffani stop to argue with a clerk about access.

She didn’t wait.

She slid into the children’s cluster and sat where there was space, knees to chest.

She looked at her hands.

Still dusty.

Still here.

**Bram POV:** He watched her peel away from them like a strip of bark coming off a branch.

His job was to keep bodies moving in the right direction.

He had moved her.

He had also shoved a thumb into a bruise he hadn’t known existed.

He told himself it was an accident.

Accidents still counted.

He followed Jhace and Tiffani through the gate funnel because that was the assignment, but his eyes kept sliding sideways to where the kids were being staged.

Chloen sat with her knees up, face turned slightly away from the crowd like she was minimizing surface area.

Bram’s brain reached for a joke because that was its only tool.

Something about kids being stacked like kindling.

He killed it before it reached his mouth.

That was new.

Jhace’s voice cut in, low. “Eyes front.”

Bram snapped his gaze back.

“Sorry,” he started.

Jhace’s eyes pinned him. “Finish it.”

Bram clenched his jaw. “Sorry like— I’m not asking forgiveness. I’m acknowledging I was stupid.” He grimaced. “And now you’re going to make me say it until it stops being funny, which is immediately.”

Tiffani said, “Stop generating noise.”

Bram glanced at her. “You talk like a notice board.”

“I prefer accuracy,” she said.

Bram wanted to say something about accuracy getting people killed if you aimed it wrong.

He didn’t.

They reached a staging lane reserved for station delegates. A clerk checked Tiffani’s slate seal and Jhace’s brace captain endorsement and waved them through.

Bram’s escort token got him through with them.

Past the gates, the amphitheater opened up.

**Camera:** Tiered stone seating. Central floor cleared. A raised speaking platform built from scaffold and braced beams. Metal horn reflectors angled inward. Rope lines dividing sections. Ledger tables at the perimeter. Guards in Crown livery at each stair throat.

Bram took three steps in and felt his humor fail to find purchase.

Too many people.

Too much quiet packed into a bowl.

He scanned for Chloen again.

He found her at the children’s wall, already absorbed into a cluster.

Her head was down.

She was alive.

He should have felt relief.

He felt guilty instead.

Because she didn’t look like someone who’d been saved.

She looked like someone who’d learned a new rule.

When Jhace and Tiffani moved deeper into the delegate lane, Bram followed.

He kept his eyes on the crowd the way a guard was supposed to.

But every time the doubled hum shifted phase, his gaze slid back to the children’s wall.

Chloen stayed small.

Bram couldn’t help thinking: I made her smaller.

He didn’t know how to undo that.

## Chapter 13 — Camera (West Riser)

The amphitheater filled by lanes.

Rope stanchions guided bodies into arcs. Guards moved along the rope with palms out, directing flow without touching. Clerks at the perimeter tables took names, station marks, family counts. Tokens changed hands: square-cut for Form directives, drop-stamped for Purity shelters, loop-marked for Wholeness kitchens, fracture-slashed for Entropy labor gangs.

The Crown’s banners hung from the upper rim where the stone was intact enough to carry weight. A square within a square repeated in cloth and etched metal.

Below the banners, the sound hardware had been assembled fast and clean.

Metal horns—riveted sheets rolled into cones—were mounted on scaffold arms and angled toward the central floor. Each arm was guyed with wire rope and braced to the stone with expansion bolts. The horns faced inward, not outward. Amplification by concentration.

At the central floor, a platform had been built from scavenged beams and pipe quarter truss pieces. The joints were over-braced. The bracing was new wood against old stone, pale and raw.

Sections of seating were marked by chalk and emblem.

Square: Crown officials, factors, station captains.

Drop: Purity orders, shelter coordinators, medics.

Loop: Wholeness harmonists, ration crews, water tables.

Fracture: Entropy crews, demolition gangs, salvage cutters.

The symbols did not keep people separated completely. Families crossed lines to reunite. Workers crossed to trade tools. Priests crossed to speak quietly with engineers.

But the symbols established initial vectors.

A city factor with rings on both hands moved along the Square section with two aides and a clerk carrying a ledger. He spoke to station captains in short bursts. Each captain answered with numbers.

Brace captains stood at the stair throats, chalk on their belts, eyes scanning stone seams and crowd loads.

Pipe quarter engineers stood in a cluster under a Loop mark, hands rubbing grit from fingertips. One held a pressure gauge and tapped its face with a knuckle, listening for a stuck needle.

A registrar table had been set up beside the Square lane, separate from the general ledger tables. Its clerks carried thicker books. The books were chained to the table legs.

A line formed there anyway.

Some people came because they wanted supplies.

Some came because they wanted protection.

Some came because they had seen stone hang in air and wanted to know if that meant they were valuable.

Guards watched the registrar line harder than the water line.

On the central floor, runners moved between sections carrying folded notices stamped with the Crown seal. Each notice used the same terminology: PSFE. Rift. Exit Face. Doubled harmonic. Pressure pulses. Resource allocation.

No notice used the word miracle.

No notice used the word curse.

At the platform edge, a speaker’s stand had been bolted down with four anchor points and a cross brace. A slate board hung behind it, blank.

When the bell cadence outside repeated—three measured, two fast, one long—the crowd noise lowered. Not silence. Compression.

A man in a clean coat stepped onto the platform.

He wore the Form emblem at the collar.

He placed both hands on the stand as if checking alignment.

He waited for the slack phase.

Then he began.

## Chapter 11 — Tiffani (Cadence)

**Camera:** Bell towers on the upper terraces. Hammered bronze mouths. Ropes vibrating. Dust lifting from parapet seams on the slack phase, settling on the pull.

Three measured strikes. Pause. Two fast. Pause. One long.

The cadence relayed down the city in delayed layers. Lower stations answered as the sequence reached them, each repeat a fraction late. The doubled harmonic bent the metal tone, but the timing held.

**Sensory intrusion:** Tiffani’s knee registered the vibration as a timing problem. Joint wanted one rhythm. Stone insisted on two.

Tiffani closed her eyes for one breath and counted the pauses. Central-station notice. Not evacuation. Not collapse alert. A call to assemble.

She was at a rope line on the upper flats, watching a crack that had been marked with wax. The seam opened a hair with each pulse and closed again on the slack phase. It had not grown in an hour. That was stability within tolerance.

She pulled her slate up, wrote the cadence in shorthand, and underlined it once. Then she followed the sound.

The nearest central station sat in a widened street cut into the terrace face—a stone amphitheater of steps and railings built for crowd control and supply handoff. The station board had been scrubbed and re-chalked twice since the Piercing. Layers of old notices ghosted beneath the new.

A runner in a dust cloak stood on the step below the board, bell rope looped at his wrist. He waited for the cadence to finish, then raised his voice so it landed between the pull and slack.

“By Crown order,” he read. “Assembly at West Riser Stadium. Briefing by Corlexi, Office of Infrastructure and Public Works. All sector leads. All brace captains. All registered medical and pipe foremen. Citizens may attend in assigned blocks. Report by bell hour three.”

He did not say why.

He did not say manifestation.

Tiffani looked at the board.

A square emblem had been stamped into the wet chalk at the header line—Form’s mark. Straight edges. Clean geometry. She recognized the administrative hand in it.

The notice itself was narrow in language.

**PUBLIC ORDER BRIEFING: PLANETARY STRUCTURAL FAILURE EVENT (PSFE).**

**COMMUNICATION STATUS: LONG-RANGE GRID COMPROMISED.**

**ASSEMBLY REQUIRED FOR RESOURCE ALLOCATION AND INSTRUCTION.**

No mention of the Rift.

No mention of the Exit Face.

No mention of the copper-gold grit that lifted against her slate edge when certain bodies moved nearby.

She wrote: *Omission pattern consistent with containment protocol.*

A brace captain stepped in close enough that his shoulder brushed her sleeve. “Glass-house?” he asked.

Tiffani didn’t turn. “Yes.”

“You going?”

“Yes.”

“Good. We need someone who can count.”

Counting was not authority. Counting was baseline.

She folded the slate under her arm and stepped out of the station flowline. She found Jhace by sound before sight—metal on stone, wedge bar bite, controlled force. He was levering a beam off a trapped cart axle while two men dragged the cart free.

His hands shook when he stopped.

When he put load through a tool, the tremor reduced.

Tiffani adjusted her path to approach on slack phase and spoke before she reached him.

“Central-station call,” she said.

Jhace didn’t look up. “Bell said that.”

“West Riser Stadium. Corlexi. Crown briefing.”

The wedge bar squealed once. Jhace set it, tested seat, then released and stood. “Form coat.”

“Yes.”

He wiped his mouth with his sleeve. The motion was automatic. The metal taste was routine now, and she tracked it by the way his jaw worked, not by the word he refused to say.

Chloen appeared from behind a stack of salvaged lumber, small and too quiet. She had been hovering within sight lines since the meeting on the upper flats. Proximity as strategy.

“We have to go?” she asked.

“Briefing,” Jhace said.

“Assembly point,” Tiffani added. “Resource allocation likely.”

Chloen’s eyes flicked to the station board. Then up, toward the Rift, and back down immediately as if looking too long could make it pull.

They moved.

The walk to West Riser was short by map and long by rubble. Streets that had been stairs were now ramps of broken slab. Rope lines cut across intersections. Brace marks—wax, chalk, red smear—turned the city into a diagram.

Tiffani kept her slate closed while moving. Open slate attracted questions.

At the final control point before the stadium access ramp, a cluster of Crown trainees stood with spear-staves and new leather harness. Their cloaks were clean enough to signal supply access. Their boots were not broken in.

One of them stepped forward like he’d been assigned the action and decided to own it.

He was young enough to still have softness at the jaw. His eyes moved too much—room-reading. Social scanning. He carried himself like a guard and spoke like a man trying to sell you a story.

“Block escort,” he said. “Name’s Bram. If you’re going to the big talk, you’re walking with me so I can pretend I’m doing my job and not just… loitering with a pointy stick.”

Jhace stopped. Looked Bram up and down. “Intern.”

Bram grinned. “I prefer ‘apprentice to systemic failure.’” He looked at the cracked terraces behind them, then at Jhace’s wedge bar. “You’re carrying that like you plan to pry the planet closed.”

Jhace didn’t move his face. “If it fits, it holds.”

Bram opened his mouth, then paused mid-start. “That— that’s actually good. I was going to say something about… wedging your hopes into a—” He shook his head as if physically dislodging the thought. “Nope. That’s stupid. Forget I started.”

Jhace’s eyes narrowed. “Finish it.”

Bram blinked. “Why?”

“Because you started.”

Tiffani watched the exchange as noise generation. Bram used humor as pressure valve. Jhace used completion as rule.

Chloen made a small sound that was not a laugh yet.

Bram sighed, surrendered. “Fine. Wedging your hopes into a crack like it’s… like it’s a—” He searched. His gaze fell on the stadium ramp supports, on the braces and rope lines. “—like it’s a brace post and it’s not going to snap when everyone leans.”

Jhace nodded once, as if the joke had been a tool and the tool had been returned to its rack. “Better.”

Tiffani’s knee pulsed. She ignored it.

Bram’s eyes shifted to her slate. “And you’re the one with the writing tablet. Great. I love being watched by people who can spell my failures.”

“I am not recording you,” Tiffani said.

“Not yet,” Bram said, and then made a face. “See? That’s also stupid. I hate my own mouth.”

Jhace’s jaw twitched. “Then stop.”

Bram looked as if the concept were new.

They started up the ramp toward the stadium entry.

The doubled harmonic underfoot kept its two-note insistence. The crowd moved in trained pulses—lean on pull, breathe on slack.

Bram walked on the outside of their small group, spear-staff angled away from bodies. He kept talking anyway.

“Corlexi’s going to tell us everything’s fine,” he said. “That the sky hole is a… a ventilation feature. You know. Improved airflow.”

Tiffani did not respond.

Bram continued, editing himself mid-sentence. “No. Not airflow. That’s— that’s… if you punch a hole in a kettle—”

“Finish it,” Jhace said, immediate.

Bram glared. “Why are you like this?”

“Because you started.”

Bram’s shoulders rose and fell. “Because you punch a hole in a kettle, the steam doesn’t politely ask where to go. It goes where it can. So now we’re the kettle. And Corlexi’s the man holding a ladle pretending he’s in charge of the boil.”

Chloen’s mouth twitched. She turned her face away as if hiding it from the air.

Tiffani noted the effect: child affect lightening. Minimal. Temporary.

At the stadium entry, Bram held up a palm to stop them while a clerk checked wrist ties. Cloth bands had been dyed by sector: blue for pipe quarter, red for brace crews, grey for unassigned civilians.

Chloen had no band.

Bram saw it and hesitated for half a beat. Then he nodded to the clerk and produced a spare strip from his belt pouch.

“Here,” he said, and tied the grey band around Chloen’s wrist without touching her skin more than necessary. “Now you’re officially a problem I’m responsible for. Congratulations.”

Jhace watched the knot. “Too loose.”

Bram sighed and retied it tighter. “Of course. Of course it is.”

Tiffani stepped forward as the line advanced. The crowd noise thickened as the stadium mouth swallowed them.

Bram fell into pace again, close enough to keep them in escort range, far enough to not crowd.

He looked at Chloen, then at the empty space beside her where an adult should have been.

He opened his mouth, then edited the joke before it landed. The correction came too late.

“So,” Bram said, too casual. “Where’d you come from? Where’s your mom? Your guardian?”

The question landed like dropped metal.

Tiffani felt her pencil fingers flex under the closed slate.

Jhace’s eyes shifted to Chloen.

Chloen stopped walking.

The doubled harmonic pulsed under them and the crowd kept moving, but the space around the girl tightened.


## Chapter 12 — Chloen / Bram (Grey Band)

Chloen kept her eyes on Bram’s boots.

Boots were easier than faces.

His boots were too new. The leather still had its shape. The soles hadn’t learned stone.

He asked the question like it was nothing.

Where’s your mom.

Your guardian.

Chloen’s throat did the tight thing.

She did not look up the mountain face. She did not look for the bend in the wall. She did not look for the missing tooth roofline. She did not look for the gap.

She told herself: *Don’t say aunt.*

If she said aunt, someone would ask where.

If she said where, she would have to point at a place that wasn’t a place anymore.

Her mouth tasted metallic again, like the air wanted a coin.

She swallowed it.

“That taste is getting old,” she thought, and hated that her head sounded like grown-ups.

Bram waited.

The crowd flowed around them. Rope lines guided bodies. A guard trainee shouted, “Keep moving,” at someone else.

Jhace didn’t speak. He stood like a brace post.

Tiffani didn’t speak. She watched like a lens.

Chloen wanted to disappear into the grey band Bram had tied on her wrist.

She chose the smallest true answer that didn’t open the rest.

“Gone,” she said.

Bram blinked once.

“Gone where?” he asked, and then immediately made a face like he wanted to claw the words back into his mouth. “No. Sorry. That— that’s not—”

Jhace’s voice cut in, flat. “Move.”

Chloen moved because moving was safer than talking.

Her feet found the slack phase because her lungs had learned it. In. Out. Don’t choke when the pull comes.

Bram fell into step beside them again. He stopped trying to be funny for three whole breaths.

Chloen counted them.

One.

Two.

Three.

On the fourth breath, Bram tried anyway.

“Okay,” he said, too careful. “Gone is… yes. Gone is a whole category. Like— like if you drop a cup and—”

Jhace turned his head a fraction. “Finish it.”

Bram made a noise that was almost a laugh and almost a cough. “No. I’m not doing this with you right now. I’m not making a dead-guardian cup joke. That’s not… that’s not a good use of my limited mouth.”

Chloen’s lips pulled up despite herself. It was a small betrayal.

She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand. Pain was simple.

They reached the stadium mouth.

The inside was loud without being chaotic. Sound had shape here. People were being sorted into wedges of seating by cloth color and brace captain callouts. A line of clerks sat at a table with ledgers and stamped tags. A rope maze guided bodies like water through channels.

Chloen hated it.

It looked too much like being processed.

A woman with an ink stamp took one look at Chloen’s grey band and pointed her toward a section where other children sat in a cluster, watched by an older volunteer with a scarf.

“Kids there,” the woman said. Not cruel. Just efficient.

Chloen didn’t want to go.

She went anyway.

She slipped away from the space between Jhace and Tiffani and walked toward the children’s section because refusing would make her visible.

Bram watched her go.

She did not look back.


**Bram**

Of course that’s how he did it.

Of course he asked the worst question in the cleanest tone like he was asking where a person bought their boots.

Bram kept his face loose while his insides did the small panic run—fast, stupid, useless. He told himself it was fine. He told himself kids were resilient. He told himself a lot of bullshit.

Chloen’s shoulders had gone tight when she said *gone*.

Gone didn’t mean moved.

Gone meant buried.

Or crushed.

Or the kind of gone that didn’t come back when you shouted a name.

He wanted to say sorry.

He didn’t.

Sorry was a word you used when you could fix the damage.

All he could do was not make it worse.

He tracked Jhace and Tiffani as they were waved toward a sector lead wedge—grey bands could sit anywhere; blue and red got priority. Of course the slate woman—Tiffani—got directed like she belonged.

Jhace looked like he’d been built to hold a door shut.

Bram knew that type. Guys who didn’t let a joke die because letting it die meant letting something else live in the silence.

He should have shut up.

He stayed quiet for five breaths.

Then the guilt found its own joke anyway, because guilt always did.

*Good job, Bram. First day escorting civilians and you step on a kid’s missing family like it’s a loose paving stone.*

He hated himself for making it funny.

He hated himself more for needing it.

At the children’s section, Chloen sat down on the stone bench without touching anyone. She made herself small and still. The volunteer with the scarf handed her a cup of water.

Chloen took it. Didn’t drink.

Bram’s chest tightened.

He stayed at the boundary rope like that mattered.

A Crown clerk called his name and waved him over. “Trainee. You’re on door control. Keep lanes open. No pushing.”

“Yes,” Bram said.

He did his job.

He watched the group move away inside the stadium bowl—Jhace following Tiffani’s shoulder line, Tiffani angling her body to spare her knee, both of them not looking back because looking back wasted time.

Chloen did not follow.

Chloen sat with the other children and stared at her hands like they belonged to someone else.

Bram’s mouth opened on a joke and shut again.

He stared at the grey band on her wrist.

He had tied it.

He had made her official.

He had asked the question.

And now she walked away from him without walking at all.

He felt guilty watching her not move.

He didn’t know what to do with it.

So he stood at the rope line, spear-staff vertical, and waited for the man in the Form coat to start talking so Bram could pretend the world still had instructions.


## Chapter 13 — Camera (Stadium)

Stone tiers rose in a half bowl cut into the terrace face. Iron strapwork reinforced the arches that held the upper benches. Rope lines divided seating into wedges. Brace posts had been driven into cracks and marked with wax.

Clerks sat at long tables near the main aisle, ledgers open. Ink stamps. Cloth bands by color. A second table held metal tokens on cords—new tags stamped with serial marks.

Guards in Crown trainee harness moved in pairs. Spear-staves angled away from bodies. They directed flow with palms and short commands.

A row of speakers had been built at the bowl’s low edge: a wooden platform braced with scavenged pipe. Behind it, a wall of angled tin panels and glass slats formed a crude sound reflector. A horn of hammered sheet metal pointed up toward the benches to amplify voice without relying on the grid.

Principle emblems appeared in the crowd on collars and pins.

Square. Drop. Loop. Fracture.

They did not gather together by emblem. They gathered by need.

A city factor’s assistants threaded through the aisles with satchels and sealed notes. Brace captains stood at the ends of rope lines, watching cracks and faces. Pipe foremen clustered near the lower benches where the doubled hum was strongest through the stone. A Purity priest moved along the outer ring, hands open, speaking low to a woman whose mouth kept tasting metal.

Glass-house observers were rare. One sat near a central aisle with a slate under her arm and a knee held stiff.

At the clerks’ tables, names were taken. Injuries listed. Missing logged. A column on one ledger page had been added in fresh ink: **ANOMALOUS LOAD EVENTS.**

A second column appeared beneath it: **WITNESS.**

The stadium filled in pulses.

On the pull phase, bodies leaned forward as if the air itself drew them.

On the slack phase, breath returned and the crowd settled.

At the entry gates, new factions formed without banners.

A brace guild line—men and women with wedge bars, chalk, wax—claimed a wedge of seats and spoke in lists.

A pipe quarter cohort—grease-stained, scarfed, carrying valve keys—claimed another and spoke in pressure cycles.

A group of clerks with identical satchels took positions at the aisle corners and watched the ledger tables more than the crowd.

A knot of young guards in trainee harness tightened their formation whenever the word **manifestation** was spoken too loudly, even though the word did not appear on any posted notice.

Above all of it, the Rift sat in the sky beyond the stadium’s open edge—a black absence framed by blue—and the crowd avoided staring at it for long.

At the platform, a man in a clean coat with a square emblem stepped up behind the reflector wall.

His hands rested on the railing. He waited for the bell sequence to quiet.

Three measured strikes. Two fast. One long.

Silence gathered.

The crowd held breath on the slack phase.

Then the man opened his mouth.
