He wanted to go back and say: sorry. He wanted to say: you don't have to answer me. He wanted to say: if you need water, I can get water.

He didn't.

Because guards didn't fix losses. Guards moved bodies.

He kept walking the lanes.

A bell struck the notice cadence again. Three short. Pause. One long. Two short.

People quieted in waves.

Bram stopped at the edge of a rope lane and watched the crowd settle into something like attention.

He watched Chloen stand when the people around her stood, then sit when they sat, matching the tide of bodies with the same careful invisibility she'd carried all day.

He watched her look once over her shoulder, not at him, just back at the path they'd walked as if checking whether the question had followed.

He felt guilty anyway.

He told himself he'd do better.

He told himself he'd ask fewer questions.

He told himself he'd keep his jokes aimed at the sky, not at kids.

Then he saw the platform at the center of the stadium and the glass horns angled toward the stands, and he felt the larger truth settle in.

Today was not going to stop handing him chances to fail.

Chapter 13 - Camera (The Bowl)

Bell stations at four corners of the stadium bowl rang the notice cadence in sequence.

Guards raised ropes to open lanes. Guards lowered ropes to close lanes.

Crowds entered by terrace ramps and split at chalk marks into sections. Cloth tabs tied to wrists matched section colors. Red, blue, grey, raw twine.

A scaffold stood at the center platform: pipe frame, strap joints, wedge pins. Glass horns were mounted on crossbars, their mouths aimed at the stone tiers. A second set of horns faced outward to carry sound beyond the stadium.

A table line formed behind the platform. Three tables. One for water cups. One for bandage cloth and salve tins. One for names.

At the names table, clerks held ledgers and charcoal sticks. A runner stood beside them with a bag of wax tabs. A guard watched the table with a hand on a baton.

Two brace captains walked the seating tiers with chalk, marking cracks and posting rope across unstable rows. A third brace captain stood near the center platform with a rolled map and a wedge bar.

Pipe quarter engineers moved along the scaffold frame, tapping pipe with a rod, listening, then tightening strap joints. One engineer carried a small pressure gauge in a glass case. The needle jittered on the doubled hum.

A forge-guild crew in soot-stained coats assembled a portable brazier near the center platform. They heated rivets. They drove them into a cracked crossbar. Steam hissed where water hit hot metal.

Priests in four emblems stood at different edges of the platform.

A square emblem on a clean cloth tab.

A droplet emblem on a dark strip of leather.

A loop emblem on braided cord.

A fractured line emblem on a torn sash.

They did not stand together.

A factor with rings on both hands took a seat at the platform rail. Two assistants stood behind him with folded papers. A crown guard captain stood at his shoulder.

A second guard captain walked the aisle lanes and pointed groups into sections. The guard captain wore a tab with the crown mark at the collar.

A group of workers in slate-dust cloaks stood near the names table. Chalk in hand. Slates under arm. They watched hands, not faces.

A message runner arrived from the upper terrace with a sealed tube. The tube was passed to a clerk, then to the factor.

The factor broke the seal. The paper was read. The paper was folded.

On the platform, an administrator in a clean coat stepped to the speaking rail. A glass horn was adjusted to align with his mouth.

At the names table, a clerk dipped a charcoal stick and wrote faster.

A bell struck once. A pause.

The stadium bowl quieted.

Chapter 14 - Tiffani (The Briefing)

The stadium was not built for this many bodies at once.

It had been built for contests.

Now the stone tiers held families and crates and brace timber. People sat with tools across their knees because putting tools down felt like letting go of the only reliable thing.

Tiffani took a position where she could see the platform and one of the main aisle lines. She kept her slate on her lap. Closed. Not because she was not recording. Because she was choosing what would not be stolen.

The horn-glass amplifiers had been assembled on the platform: three flared cones of smoked glass mounted in iron frames, their throats wrapped in cloth to reduce vibration. Copper wire bracing ran from the frames to anchors drilled into the stone.

The wire shivered on the doubled hum.

A bell cadence ran again from the nearest station tower, shorter this time, a confirmation sequence.

Three short. One long. One short.

Attention.

A crown guard line tightened along the platform edge. Interns were mixed in with full guards. Bram was easy to identify by absence of stillness. He shifted, then corrected, then shifted again like he was trying to find a posture that did not look like a boy wearing someone else's authority.

Jhace stood one tier down and two rows over, wedge bar tucked under his arm like a crutch he refused to admit he needed. Chloen was beside him, shoulders drawn in, eyes scanning for exits even though this place had too many.

A man stepped onto the platform.

Clean coat. Straight seams. An iron emblem pinned at the collar: a square frame with a single diagonal bar. Form.

He did not wait for applause.

He placed both hands on the podium rail and began.

"Notice from the Crown Council on Infrastructure and Public Safety," he said.

The horn-glass carried his voice across stone. It arrived in fragments where the hum interfered and clean where the cloth wraps held.

"Chief Administrator Corlexi," he continued. "Office of Infrastructure and Public Works. Forty-eight hour situation report. Verified items only."

Tiffani opened her slate.

She wrote the time interval in the corner and drew a line under it.

Corlexi spoke in numbered sections.

"One: Communication status. The long-range grid is nonfunctional in most territories." He paused, allowing the crowd to hear the phrase most territories and supply its own meaning. "Metal infrastructure is carrying the new harmonic. It is not usable for transmission. Attempts to force signal through have resulted in line fracture and fire."

Tiffani wrote: GRID 98% FAIL (REPORT). CONDUCTOR BEHAVIOR.

"Local bell networks remain effective within city limits. Cadence corruption has been observed. If your station tower issues overlapping sequences, defer to the longest strike. The long strike indicates verified notice."

A brace captain two tiers down nodded once, sharp, repeating the instruction to the men near him.

"Physical messengers are primary method between settlements," Corlexi said. "Expect delays. Expect distortion. Do not treat single reports as fact."

Tiffani added: MESSENGER LAG 12-48H. TRAUMA NOISE.

Corlexi's mouth did not change shape on the word trauma. His voice did not change either.

"Glass-house observatories are currently the only reliable source of baseline physical measurements," he continued. "If you have an observatory in your district, it is now a Crown data asset. Protect it."

Tiffani felt her knee register the doubled hum and ignored it.

She underlined: DATA ASSET.

"Two: Event characterization. The Crown confirms a high-velocity iron-glass projectile transited Te'Oga on a linear path."

A murmur lifted through the tiers. Not disbelief. The sound of people hearing a sentence shaped like permission to be afraid.

Corlexi held his hands steady on the rail.

"Entry and exit sites are under assessment. Multiple atmospheric discontinuities have been observed." He did not say Rift. He did not say sky wound. He did not say stars in daylight. "A sustained harmonic vibration persists across the crust. This is now the baseline."

Tiffani wrote: LINEAR TRANSIT CONFIRMED (CROWN). MULTIPLE DISCONTINUITY. HARMONIC = BASELINE.

"Three: Infrastructure. Structural failure is ongoing. Liquefaction effects have been reported in multiple regions. Buildings have re-set under load. Do not assume a wall is stable because it is standing."

Jhace shifted his wedge bar to a new grip. His hands stayed low.

"Mark cracks. Rope off edges. Use brace timber. If you do not have brace timber, use pipe. If you do not have pipe, use stone wedges."

Corlexi's voice was not inspirational. It was a checklist.

"Four: Population control and resource allocation. All citizens are to report to Assembly Points for ration distribution and work assignment."

A factor in the lower tier lifted his chin at that, as if he recognized his own authority being reinforced.

"Assembly Points will also serve as census nodes. Names, household counts, injuries, skills."

Tiffani wrote: CENSUS NODES.

Her graphite slowed for one beat.

"Five: Anomalous load-bearing incidents."

The crowd shifted.

Corlexi did not look toward Jhace. He did not need to.

"Some districts have reported short-duration structural stabilization events inconsistent with available brace methods. If you have witnessed or experienced such an incident, do not attempt to repeat it in public spaces."

He let the phrase inconsistent with available brace methods hang.

"Report to your nearest Assembly Point for assessment. This is not punishment. This is hazard control."

Hazard control.

Tiffani wrote it exactly.

Corlexi's eyes moved over the stadium tiers like he was scanning a ledger.

"The Crown will hold daily briefings at central stations. Verification requires collected data. Speculation will be treated as a destabilizing force."

Form principle in a single sentence: reduce unknowns, enforce shape.

He did not mention the orbital debris band.

He did not mention the hole through the planet.

He did not mention the way the grit lifted along slate edges near certain bodies.

He did not mention the ocean turning.

He said, "We are alive. We are still functional. We will restore order through measured work."

Then he stepped back from the podium.

A crown guard approached the platform stairs. A registrar with a ledger moved into position at a side table.

The crowd began to stand.

Stone shifted under thousands of feet.

The hum tightened.

Tiffani felt it before she could name it. The slack phase did not arrive on schedule.

Her knee caught on the expected beat and found none.

A missed step.

A stutter.

Breath pulled shallow across the tiers in the same instant, as if the stadium had become one body and somebody had squeezed.

Metal in mouths. Not copper. A hard clean hit.

A brace timber in the lower aisle line creaked.

Then the world skipped.

Not an earthquake. Not a ring.

A discontinuity.

Bram was on the aisle line, one hand on a rope post. His posture went wrong for a fraction, like a puppet whose string had slackened and snapped tight again. He recovered without falling. He did not understand what had happened. His eyes widened and then locked back into his assigned scan.

A worker carrying a crate did not recover.

His foot landed on a stone lip that had been stable one beat ago. The lip shifted under the stutter. The crate tilted. His weight followed.

He went down.

The crate broke.

Glass shards and iron bolts scattered.

The worker's forearm struck a jagged edge.

Blood hit stone.

Tiffani's pencil moved without permission.

BLOOD. LOCATION: AISLE 3. CAUSE: PHASE NULL / LOAD SHIFT.

She wrote around the red as if it were a stain on the page. Her lines tightened. Her letters narrowed.

The hum returned.

The slack phase arrived late.

Air rushed back into lungs. The crowd noise surged, then splintered into shouts.

Guards moved. Brace captains moved. Two medics pushed through with a cloth roll and a splint.

Bram's face went pale for one heartbeat, then he stepped aside to make a lane. He did not joke. He did not freeze. He held the rope post steady while bodies passed.

Jhace did not move toward the blood. He watched the brace timber and the shifting stone and the way the stutter had changed the crowd's balance. His hands flexed once, then clenched.

Chloen stood with her arms tight around herself, eyes on the aisle line. Her mouth worked once as if the metallic taste was too loud.

Corlexi remained on the platform.

He did not return to the podium.

He did not speak.

His clean coat did not change.

A registrar leaned in to say something to him. Corlexi nodded once, sharp, and pointed toward the side table.

The ledger opened.

Names began to be taken.

Tiffani closed her slate.

Not because the blood was not data.

Because the act of writing had just become part of the event.

She could feel the graphite under her fingers like a conductor.

She stood and shifted one step to spare her knee.

She did not look at Bram.

She did not look at Jhace.

She looked at the horn-glass amplifiers and the copper wire bracing and the way the wire shivered on the baseline as if it was listening.

Then she exhaled on the slack phase and moved toward the aisle line where the blood had been, because measurement required proximity and proximity now carried cost.

Chapter 15 – Tiffani (The Omission)

The bells rang the Assembly cadence.

Not alarm. Not evacuation. Not mourning.

Instruction.

Three low. One high. Pause. Then the repeat—slightly off, like everything else now. The harmonic bled into the bronze, stretched the spacing between notes until the rhythm felt learned rather than remembered.

Tiffani stood where she could see the whole of it without being seen herself.

The crowd had been arranged for sound, not comfort. That was deliberate. Stone risers angled shallow toward the dais. Rope lines kept bodies from pressing too close to the glasswork that carried the amplification. Brace captains flanked the aisles, hands loose, eyes working.

People had stopped talking before the bells finished.

That mattered.

They were waiting to be told what the Event was.

Corlexi stood already at the center of the platform when the last echo died. No procession. No flourish. He wore Crown gray—clean lines, no ornament—and the way he held himself said he had practiced stillness until it read as calm instead of fear.

Tiffani opened her slate.

Not to write yet. To feel the grit along the edge lift and settle. Baseline check. It answered.

Corlexi began.

“Citizens of Saint Merro and the upper terraces,” he said, voice carried clean through the glass horns. “We are convened under conditions without precedent.”

That was true. Also useless.

“We have experienced a planetary structural failure event,” he continued. “External in origin. Sudden. Unpreventable.”

External. Tiffani underlined it in her mind and waited for the evidence that would follow.

“It has compromised the grid,” Corlexi said. “Altered pressure dynamics. Introduced sustained harmonic instability.”

He was careful with verbs. Passive constructions. No actor named.

The crowd stayed quiet. Too quiet. People leaned forward a fraction with each pulse, then settled back on the slack. They had learned the rhythm faster than she’d expected.

Corlexi spoke of logistics.

Assembly points. Rationing. Repair priorities. The need for order while assessment continued. He named the glass-houses—plural—and acknowledged the Underface without promising aid he couldn’t deliver.

All of it was competent.

All of it was insufficient.

Tiffani waited for him to say how big.

She waited for where else.

She waited for him to acknowledge the hole that ran through the planet like a throat.

He did not.

Around her, reactions registered in micro-movements.

Relief, where people heard “external” and translated it to not our fault.

Anxiety, where “assessment ongoing” sounded like no answers yet.

Bram stood two rows down, assigned to the aisle. He leaned on his spear like it was a prop, jaw tight, eyes bright with the effort of paying attention. He nodded at the right places. When Corlexi spoke about “citizen cooperation,” Bram’s shoulders eased, just a touch.

Jhace did not nod.

He stood back from the risers, weight on his heels, arms folded like he was bracing a wall. His gaze never left Corlexi’s hands. Not his face. His hands.

Chloen sat on the stone step between two adults who weren’t hers. Her feet didn’t touch the ground. She swung them in time with the slack phase, then stopped when she noticed herself doing it.

Corlexi moved to containment.

“There have been reports,” he said, carefully, “of anomalous load-bearing responses during the event.”

A ripple went through the crowd. Not noise. Attention.

“These responses are under review,” Corlexi continued. “They appear to be temporary, situational, and costly to the individuals involved.”

Costly. True. Still incomplete.

“They are not weapons,” he said. “They are not signs. They are not gifts.”

Tiffani’s pencil hovered.

He didn’t say what they were.

She watched the factor’s ringed hand tighten on the rail. Watched a pipe engineer glance down at his own fingers like he expected them to glow. Watched Bram straighten, relief clear on his face at the word not weapons.

Jhace’s jaw set.

Corlexi spoke for twelve minutes.

Tiffani counted.

Twelve minutes of answers that stabilized, and one omission that grew heavier with every breath.

He never said permanent.

He never said through.

He never said elsewhere.

He never said this will happen again.

When he concluded, he thanked them for their restraint.

For their patience.

For their trust.

Trust, Tiffani thought, was a measurement you only noticed after it failed.

Corlexi stepped back from the glass horns. Applause started—tentative, scattered, then gathering momentum as people followed the first claps like a safety rail.

Bram clapped. Once. Then again.

Tiffani didn’t.

Corlexi inclined his head, turned, and walked off the platform along the side ramp. As he did, he glanced—not toward the crowd, not toward the glass-houses—but toward the far edge of the dais.

Toward a woman already standing.

Eries did not wait for permission.

She did not raise her voice.

She did not step into the amplification.

She simply spoke, and the room leaned toward her as if sound had weight.

“Administrator Corlexi,” she said, clear and calm, “has thanked you for your trust.”

Corlexi continued offstage.

Eries faced the crowd.

“And I would like to thank you,” she went on, “for your silence.”

Tiffani felt the room change shape.

Not louder. Not quieter.

Aligned.

Eries smiled—not kindly, not cruelly. Precisely. Like someone who knew the cost of the truth and intended to spend it anyway.

“And now,” Eries said, “I would like to speak about what we have not yet named.”

Tiffani’s pencil touched the slate.

And for the first time since the bells rang, she did not know whether she was about to record data—

—or watch it be taken.

Chapter 16 – Chloen (The Easy Answer)

Chloen saw it before the words changed.

Not the woman. Not the sound.

The slate.

Tiffani’s hand moved the way it always did right before something mattered. Not fast. Not careful. Just… ready. Like the slate had weight and she was deciding whether to lift it or let it stay where it was.

Chloen watched the edge of it. The grit there lifted a little, then settled. Lifted again.

She felt it in her teeth.

The lady on the platform hadn’t stepped into the loud-glass, but her voice still got everywhere. It slid into the space between people and sat there like it belonged.

“And now,” the lady said, “I would like to speak about what we have not yet named.”

People leaned without noticing.

Chloen leaned too, then stopped when she realized she was doing it and hugged her elbows instead. She didn’t want to move unless she meant it.

The lady—Eries, someone whispered nearby, like names were currency now—looked like she’d always been standing there. Dark dress. Bright pin at the collar, shaped like a broken loop. She smiled like she was sorry for everyone, but not enough to stop.

“You’ve been told this was external,” Eries said. “Unpreventable. Finished.”

Her eyes moved across the crowd, slow and even, like she was counting heads without numbers.

“I am here to tell you,” she continued, “that this is not over—and pretending otherwise is not stability. It’s delay.”

Chloen felt a small, sharp click inside her chest.

Delay was a word you used when something bad was coming back.

Around her, grown-ups shifted. Some nodded. Some crossed their arms like that might hold something in.

Eries didn’t raise her voice.

“You have felt the cost,” she said. “In your mouths. In your joints. In your breath. You have seen stone obey hands. You have seen water turn.”

Chloen thought of the wave. The way it bent like it had listened.

Jhace didn’t move. He looked like a wall someone had leaned on too long.

Bram did move. Just a little. Like he’d been holding something in and finally found a place to put it.

“That frightens you,” Eries said. “It should.”

Chloen frowned.

That wasn’t how you talked if you were trying to help.

“But fear,” Eries went on, “is not the enemy. Confusion is.”

Tiffani’s pencil hovered. Not writing. Waiting.

Chloen wanted to tap her. To say don’t. She didn’t know why. She just knew the slate felt heavier than it should.

“You are not broken,” Eries said. “And this is not punishment.”

That made someone near Chloen make a small sound. Relief, maybe. Or wanting to believe.

“This,” Eries said, spreading her hands, “is entropy made visible. Ruin does not mean evil. It means change without permission.”

Chloen didn’t like the way that sounded tidy.

Eries smiled again.

“And change,” she said, “can be guided.”

There it was.

The easy part.

“You don’t need to understand it,” Eries continued. “You don’t need to measure it. You need to stop fighting what is already happening.”

Chloen’s fingers curled into her sleeves.

Stop fighting.

Like when the slab fell.

Like when you didn’t scream because screaming didn’t help.

Bram nodded. Not all the way. Just enough that Chloen saw it.

Tiffani did not nod.

But she didn’t lower the slate either.

Jhace’s mouth tightened. He shook his head once, sharp, like knocking something loose.

Eries saw him. Chloen was sure of it. But she didn’t look at him. She looked at the crowd instead.

“There are those among you,” Eries said, “who have already proven capable. Not through training. Not through doctrine. Through instinct.”

Chloen’s stomach dipped.

“You don’t need registries,” Eries went on smoothly. “You don’t need lists. You don’t need to be afraid of being counted.”

Chloen thought of the woman with the chalk. The counting. The eyes.

Eries’ voice softened.

“You need support. Structure. Permission.”

That word slid into Chloen like a splinter.

Permission was something adults gave you so they didn’t have to watch.

Bram shifted his spear to his other hand. He looked at Tiffani, quick, like he wanted to check something.

“This isn’t about power,” Eries said. “It’s about responsibility.”

Chloen snorted before she could stop herself. It came out small, like a breath that tripped.

The woman next to her glanced down. Chloen stared at the stone until the woman looked away.

Tiffani’s pencil touched the slate.

Just touched.

Didn’t move.

Chloen saw the moment stretch. Like the space between one count and the next when you didn’t know if the rhythm would come back.

Eries finished gently.

“We can build something that holds,” she said. “Together. Or we can pretend the ground isn’t still moving.”

Silence sat on the crowd.

Then someone clapped.

Then someone else.

Bram didn’t clap, but his shoulders loosened like he’d set a pack down.

Tiffani finally wrote.

Just one line.

Chloen couldn’t see the words, but she saw the graphite move around a darker smear already on the slate, like water avoiding oil.

Jhace turned away.

Not far. Just enough.

Like he was done listening.

Chloen stared at Eries and tried to figure out what felt wrong.

It wasn’t the words. The words made sense.

It was the way they fit too easily. Like a puzzle with pieces shaved down so they’d stop resisting.

Chloen looked at the emblems stitched into coats and pinned to collars across the crowd. Square. Drop. Loop. Fracture.

They weren’t where they’d been before.

She didn’t know how she knew that.

She just did.

Chloen swallowed and tasted metal again—duller now, like it had been there a while.

She leaned toward Tiffani’s side, not touching her, just close enough that if Tiffani moved, she’d feel it.

“Don’t,” Chloen whispered. Not loud enough for the slate. Just for the space between them.

Tiffani didn’t look down.

But her pencil stopped.

And Chloen watched Eries smile wider, like she’d felt the resistance and counted it anyway.

Chapter 17 — Tiffani (Deployment)

Camera (baseline): The stadium held a bowl of bodies. Rope lanes cut the stone flats into corridors. Bell towers at three stations rang cadence through metal throats. Registry tables formed a spine under awnings. Water barrels sat in rows. A thin ash haze drifted and then reversed, as the air pulled and slacked.

Sensory intrusion: The doubled hum sat in the knee joint like an offset gear. Timing problem. Two beats where there should have been one.

Tiffani stood where she could see the lanes and the stage without being part of either.

She kept her slate tucked, not open. Open invited requests. Requests turned observation into labor. Labor made her miss the drift.

Bells rang again—short, short, long—then a pause, then the same sequence repeated. Not panic cadence. Notice cadence. Central-station call.

People reacted like trained metal. Heads lifted. Feet adjusted. Bodies shifted toward the lanes without anyone shouting at them.

That was new.

That was the first clean thing she’d seen since the sky tore.

Bram moved along the nearest rope line with three other crown interns—too young for the way they were trying to carry authority, too stubborn to drop it. He had a sash pinned wrong and kept re-seating it as if fabric could be corrected into competence.

He leaned down to a woman with a wrapped arm and pointed toward the nearest water barrel. His mouth moved. The woman nodded, then laughed once—short, unwilling. Bram’s shoulders loosened like he’d removed a pressure pin.

Jhace stood off the line, wedge bar still in hand. Not working now. Watching. Refusing to be slotted into a lane.

Chloen remained close enough to Tiffani that the crowd didn’t swallow her. Close enough that Tiffani could track her without turning it into a promise. The girl’s eyes kept jumping—rope, table, stage, back to rope—like she was mapping exits.

The stage had been a platform for games. It was now a point of control.

Corlexi had stepped down and taken a side position near the rear struts, half-shadowed by hanging banners. Form emblem at his collar. Clean coat. Too clean for the day. His posture read administrative fatigue as if it were a uniform requirement.

Eries stood in the center.

High class by cut and fit alone—no dust-stained hem, no patched seams, no improvised wrap. She wore a mantle that could have been ceremonial if it hadn’t been practical wool. That was the trick. She looked like she belonged and like she’d come to help.

She didn’t raise her hands for silence. She waited until the bell cadence finished and let the last long note die.

The stadium quieted on its own.

Tiffani catalogued the quiet like a structural event.

Eries spoke without amplification and still carried.

“Your air is changing,” she said, and didn’t dress it in myth. “It will keep changing. The pull and slack will continue until the planet settles into a new baseline.”

Baseline. Useful word. Correct enough to calm. Vague enough to hide scope.

Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched. She kept it still.

Eries continued, “You will not survive this by arguing with it.”

She turned her head slightly, letting her gaze travel across the bowl so people could feel seen without being singled out.

“Breathing protocols will be taught at every station,” Eries said. “The bell sequences will mark phase. When the cadence is short-short-long: shallow. When it is long-long-short: fill. You will practice. It will become habit. Habit is how bodies stop panicking.”

Bram nodded before she finished. Not a conscious decision. Reflex agreement to a pattern that promised control.

Jhace’s jaw set.

Tiffani did not approve or reject. She evaluated.

Bell cadence as phase training.

If the cadence stayed true through harmonic interference, it could work. If it corrupted, it could kill.

Eries didn’t stop there.

“The grit,” she said, and a ripple moved through the crowd at the word. People touched their mouths. Their sleeves. Their tongues.

Eries lifted a strip of cloth from her mantle—white, layered, folded tight. Not clean linen. Filter cloth. Industrial.

“Wear it over your mouth,” she said. “Wet it. Replace it. The metallic particulate is conductive. It is in your lungs. It is in your teeth. We will reduce load.”

Load. She was speaking their language on purpose.

Tiffani’s attention sharpened. That wasn’t a priest’s vocabulary. That was infrastructure.

Eries looked down at the first row of registry tables and pointed with two fingers, not dramatic, just directional.

“Assembly points are now allocation points,” she said. “Food, water, heat access, brace crews. You will move through the lanes. You will be marked. You will be counted.”

Counted.

Tiffani watched the crowd absorb that.

Some faces tightened. Some relaxed.

Fear and relief were the same muscle, apparently. They just pulled in different directions.

Eries added, “Those who experienced anomaly—those who held load, suspended objects, redirected flow—will be asked to step to the left lane. Not to punish you. To protect you.”

Protect you. The softest lie. The most effective one.

Tiffani’s knee registered a sharper pulse. She adjusted weight and pretended it was nothing.

Chloen made a small noise behind her teeth—almost a snort, caught and swallowed.

Jhace didn’t move toward any lane.

Bram did. Half a step, then stopped like he remembered he wasn’t supposed to volunteer himself. He looked sideways toward Tiffani as if she were the adult who could tell him if it was safe.

Tiffani did not give him that.

Eries kept speaking, voice even.

“Not everyone will manifest,” she said. “Not every anomaly is repeatable. We will not encourage attempts. We will not allow spectacle. We will stabilize first.”

That line landed.

People wanted rules. They wanted permission to stop being terrified of their own bodies.

Eries gave it to them.

Corlexi’s mouth tightened at the word allow.

Tiffani tracked him without looking directly. Peripheral only. He was still. Too still.

Eries shifted—subtle—but her stance widened a fraction, like she was bracing on a new load path.

“And I will say what was not said,” Eries continued, and the stadium leaned in.

Tiffani felt it like a pressure change.

“The Crown does not have a full picture,” Eries said. “The grid has failed. Long-range communication is compromised. Reports are delayed. Distorted. Traumatized.”

The word traumatized was a concession. It made her sound human. It also invalidated messengers in advance.

“Which means,” Eries said, “we cannot wait for certainty. We cannot wait for perfect data. We cannot wait for someone else to tell us what happened.”

Corlexi’s eyes cut toward her. Sharp. Controlled.

Anger, but contained. Like steam in a pipe you didn’t want to rupture in public.

Eries didn’t glance his way. She didn’t need to. The audience did the work for her by assuming Corlexi had held back out of cowardice or calculation.

Eries raised her hand and held up four fingers.

“Form,” she said. “Purity. Wholeness. Entropy.”

The emblems on banners around the stadium caught light at the mention—four marks that had always been decoration. Now they were being used as levers.

“We have fought our contradictions for Anneals,” Eries said. “We will keep fighting them. That is the engine. But today is not doctrine day. Today is survival day.”

Clever. She disarmed theological resistance by pretending to defer it.

Tiffani watched Bram again.

He looked relieved to be given permission to not think.

He also looked grateful.

That was the problem.

Eries finished with logistics—routes, station names, brace assignment signals, bell translations. She spoke in lists without sounding like a clerk.

A woman in the mid-bowl shouted, “Will the sky close?”

Eries didn’t flinch. “We do not know,” she said. “We will not lie to you. We will build as if it does not.”

Truth, selected. Useful truth.

A man shouted, “Will the ocean come again?”

Eries paused just long enough to be seen pausing.

“Water will move,” she said. “We will put eyes on the lower basins. We will establish watchers. We will sound warning cadence early.”

No promise. Just structure. The crowd swallowed it like medicine.

Tiffani hated how effective it was.

Eries turned, finally, toward Corlexi—barely—just enough to include him in her closing.

“Administrator Corlexi will coordinate infrastructure through the Crown stations,” she said. “He will oversee brace crews and grid stabilization.”

A gift and a leash in one sentence.

Corlexi’s jaw tightened harder. He gave a shallow nod because refusing would be visible.

Eries smiled without warmth. Then she stepped back half a pace, signaling the end.

Applause started in the bowl—hesitant, then spreading, because people needed an action that wasn’t screaming.

Tiffani watched the applause become consent.

Corlexi moved toward the stage steps as if the floor had turned to hot metal under him.

As he passed the banner strut, he looked back—just once—at Eries.

No words.

Just a look with enough contained heat to light a forge.

Then he continued off stage and vanished behind the awnings.

Eries stayed in the center.

She lifted her chin slightly and let the crowd keep applauding like it was their idea.

Tiffani’s fingers itched to write.

Not because she trusted any of it.

Because this—this moment, this shift—was the first true structural change since the Event.

Stone could be braced.

Air could be timed.

People could be organized.

And a system that worked in crisis could become permanent faster than any crack in a wall.

Chloen leaned closer, barely a movement, and whispered without looking at Tiffani’s face.

“She made it easy.”

Tiffani did not answer.

Bram exhaled like a man who’d been holding breath for two days. He looked at Jhace, half-grinning, and said something under his breath that didn’t quite become a joke.

Jhace didn’t smile.

He turned away from the lanes, wedge bar still in hand, and walked toward the edge of the stadium where shadows broke lines and no one could tag him with chalk.

Refusal was a choice too.

Tiffani finally opened her slate.

She wrote one line. Only one.

Deployment begun. Effect: immediate compliance. Unknown cost.

Chapter 18 — Chloen (Habituation)

Chloen didn’t taste metal anymore.

She noticed it the way you noticed when a sore tooth stopped hurting—by the wrongness of the quiet where the ache had been.

She pressed her tongue to the back of her teeth and waited for the familiar sharpness. Nothing. Just the flat taste of damp cloth and old air.

That should have been good.

It made her stomach tighten anyway.

The stadium was louder now, but calmer. The noise had changed shape. Less shouting. More calling. Names, numbers, directions. The sound of people being told where to stand and believing it.

Bells rang again—long, long, short—and everyone around her breathed in together like a trick they’d always known.

Chloen copied them. She didn’t mean to. Her chest filled on time.

That was new too.

The rope lanes worked. People moved through them without fighting. Brace crews appeared where walls leaned wrong. Someone had figured out how to stack broken stone so it didn’t scrape when the pull came. Fires near the outer ring had gone down to steam and smoke.

The world was behaving.

Chloen didn’t trust it.

She stayed close to Tiffani’s side, just behind the place where adults forgot to look. Close enough to feel the slate shift when Tiffani moved. The slate had weight now. Not physical weight. Meaning-weight.

Tiffani kept writing in short bursts, then stopping. Writing, stopping. Like she was afraid the words would run ahead of her.

Bram moved along the lanes again, more confident this time. Someone had fixed his sash. It sat right now. He looked like he belonged in it, which scared Chloen more than when he’d looked like he didn’t.

He crouched in front of a man with shaking hands and said something gentle. The man nodded and laughed, the same short laugh as before. Bram laughed back, softer, and helped him stand.

“See?” Bram said to no one in particular. “It works.”

Chloen looked down at the stone so she didn’t have to look at Bram’s face when he said it.

Jhace wasn’t in the bowl anymore.

She noticed that absence the way she noticed missing stairs—by almost stepping where something should have been.

The emblems on coats and banners had finished settling. Square near the front. Loop nearer the lanes. Drop along the water tables. Fracture pushed outward, closer to the exits.

That wasn’t how they’d been.

Chloen didn’t know how she knew the difference. She just knew the shapes had moved the way animals moved when you weren’t watching them directly.

A brace captain called for hands near the west rib. Chloen drifted with the crowd until she wasn’t near Tiffani anymore, then stopped.

No one noticed.

That was the trick.

She stood where two rope lanes crossed and watched how people flowed around the knot. One lane moved faster. The other slowed and backed up. People grew impatient and leaned, and the lean pushed the timing off by half a beat.

Half a beat mattered now.

Chloen stepped sideways.

Just one pace. Not dramatic. Just enough to stand where the knot wanted to form.

The next pull came.

People hesitated. The lane loosened. The knot didn’t catch.

No one fell.

No one noticed her.

Chloen’s heart thudded too fast, then slowed when the slack phase came like it was supposed to.

She hadn’t meant to do anything.

She just didn’t like how the ropes were talking to the crowd.

She drifted back toward the stone flats and found the low wall again. Sat. Hugged her elbows. Checked her mouth.

Still no metal.

That was worse.

Tiffani passed close enough that Chloen could hear the pencil scratch. The graphite moved cleanly now, not skirting smears, not hesitating.

Chloen wanted to say something. Anything.

She didn’t.

Across the stadium, Eries moved among the lanes without hurry. People made space for her without being told. She nodded. She listened. She touched shoulders lightly and left them straighter than before.

She didn’t look at Chloen.

Which meant she knew exactly where Chloen was.

The bells rang notice cadence again—short, short, long—and this time Chloen breathed shallow before she remembered she was supposed to.

Her body had learned faster than she had.

She pressed her thumb into her palm until it hurt, just to make sure pain still worked the same way.

It did.

Good.

A woman nearby whispered, “It’s better already,” and someone else answered, “Thank the Crown,” and a third said, “Thank her,” without needing to name who.

Chloen watched the words move through the air like dust settling into cracks.

She licked her teeth again.

Nothing.

She imagined the taste was still there, just buried deeper. Like grit ground into stone so fine you didn’t feel it until later.

Chloen slid off the wall and followed the edge of the stadium where shadows cut the lines and the bells sounded farther away.

She didn’t know what would happen next.

She only knew this:

If the world stopped hurting you right away, it didn’t mean it was healed.

Sometimes it meant you’d stopped feeling the damage.

Chloen kept walking, quiet and uncounted, while the city learned how to behave.

Epilogue — Corlexi

Corlexi did not return to the floor.

He took the side stair instead—the one cut narrow for servants and clerks, stone worn smooth by centuries of people who were never meant to be seen leaving power. The bells were still ringing when he reached the landing, their cadence settling into something regular enough to pretend it had always been that way.

He paused with one hand on the rail.

Listened.

The doubled hum had stabilized. Not vanished. Not weakened. Trained. The city had learned the rhythm the way a body learned a limp—by surviving it long enough to forget there had ever been another gait.

Corlexi exhaled through his nose.

Of course it had.

He removed his coat.

The fabric peeled away from his shoulder with a faint sound, like static breaking contact. Beneath it, his shirt was damp. Not with sweat.

Copper had bloomed along the seam under his collarbone, dark and wet, already crusting at the edges where the air touched it. He pressed two fingers there, gently, as if checking the integrity of a joint.

The pain answered cleanly.

Good.

He had stepped down before Eries spoke. That had been deliberate. The Crown understood exits. They understood optics. Letting her take the floor wasn’t surrender; it was allocation.

Let her name what he had not.

Let her make it palatable.

Corlexi folded the coat over his arm, careful not to smear the inside. The stain would need addressing later. The Council disliked visible costs.

From below, the crowd noise rose and fell—not panic now, not confusion. Something closer to agreement. The sound people made when the world offered them a way to stop asking questions.

He closed his eyes for a single count.

He saw the wave again—not the one the city had watched, but the earlier one, the first impossible pull. The moment the harmonic had opened and his body had answered before his doctrine could object.

He had felt it then:

the access.

the line.

the invitation.

He had chosen containment.

Eries had chosen momentum.

Corlexi opened his eyes.

Anger moved through him—not hot, not wild. Cold. Precise. The kind that filed itself down and waited for the correct application.

He listened to the bells one last time.

The cadence held.

People would follow it.

He adjusted his grip on the folded coat and continued down the stair, out of sight, while Eries’s voice carried across the stadium behind him—clear, confident, perfectly reasonable.

The city did not know it had chosen.

That was fine.

Choices made without knowledge were still choices.

And they still came due.

[ASSISTANT]
You’ve got **two different books welded together** in here:

1) **Chs 1–10** = raw Event, tactile engineering-horror, tight POV discipline.  
2) **Chs 11–18 + Epilogue** = civic machinery, ideology, bureaucracy, narrative-control war.

That pivot is *fine*—it’s actually the correct pivot for your thesis—but right now the weld is sloppy, which is why you felt “lost” and why “it doesn’t match older chapters.”

Here’s what’s working, what’s off, and what to do next (without rewriting the whole damn thing).

---

## What’s working (keep it)

### 1) Your baseline voice is strong
Jhace’s chapters read like **load paths and guilt**. Tiffani reads like **instrument-grade sanity**. Chloen reads like **child logic surviving adult lies**. Mobel is **religion with a knife edge**.

That’s the spine.

### 2) The glass/forge punk texture is real
Pipe ribs, strap joints, glass horns, iron frames, brace timber, wedges—this world feels built, not invented.

### 3) Eries is dangerous because she *works*
She’s not “evil speech lady.” She’s the person who makes the crowd go: *thank god, someone competent.*

That’s your antagonist.

---

## What’s not working (and why it’s tripping you)

### 1) You accidentally repeated your “first 10 chapters” as if they’re canon
You pasted Chapters 1–10 **as full narrative**, then later you wrote chapters that refer to earlier drafts (Bram intro, Corlexi briefing, etc.) but those beats aren’t aligned cleanly.

Result: the reader (and you) feels like the story is restarting and re-explaining itself.

**Fix:** Decide which “version” is canon for Chs 1–10 and lock it. Anything not canon becomes scraps.

### 2) POV discipline breaks around Chapters 11–13
Chapter 11 says “Tiffani” but functions like a stage setup for Bram and the crowd. Then Chapter 12 becomes hybrid POV. Then Chapter 13 becomes camera.

That can work—**if it’s intentional cadence**—but right now it reads like uncertainty.

**Fix:** Make a rule and stick to it:
- Either: “Camera chapters are numbered and clearly labeled, and never pretend to be a character POV,”
- Or: “Camera is always interstitial (no chapter number), like a dossier insert.”

Pick one.

### 3) Corlexi’s speech is clean, but the chapter’s *purpose* isn’t
Chapter 14 does a lot. 15 tries to be “the omission.” 16 is Eries seduction through Chloen. That’s a good trio.

But you later inserted **17–18** and then the epilogue, and those feel like an alternate ending layer rather than inevitability.

**Fix:** Either end the novella at **16 + epilogue**, or end at **18 + epilogue**, but right now you have both endings fighting.

### 4) The “blood on the slate” beat is still haunting this draft
You said you don’t want death or injury—fine. But you still have:
- worker injury in Chapter 14 stutter
- “blood hit stone” / “blood on slate” energy

It’s not a problem to have injury; it’s a problem that you emotionally framed it like it’s the thesis moment, then you backed away from that commitment.

**Fix:** If you don’t want blood-as-thesis, then the thesis moment should be **Eries turning compliance into ritual**, not injury.

---

## The clean novella-ending arc you already built (you just need to choose it)

You have a satisfying arc that closes a novella while leaving a series hook:

### Novella arc closure:
- **Event happens**
- **People discover “hold/redirect”**
- **Crown tries to narrate**
- **Eries hijacks narration**
- **System stabilizes locally**
- **Main cast splinters in response**
- **Corlexi exits with hidden cost**

That’s a full arc.

### Series hook:
- Eries becomes infrastructure.
- Corlexi is not “weak,” he’s **containing something**.
- The stabilized cadence is a **trained limp** (great line/idea).
- Chloen noticing emblem re-ordering = omen.

---

## What happens now (concrete next moves)

### Option A (tightest): End at Chapter 16 + Epilogue
- 15: Corlexi omission  
- 16: Eries seduction (Chloen sees through it first)  
- Epilogue: Corlexi cost + anger + “choices come due”

This ends sharp and leaves a hook without bloat.

### Option B (more political): Keep 17–18 but compress their function
If you keep 17–18, they should **do exactly one job**:
- 17 shows Eries’s system immediately working (breathing cadence, lanes, filter cloth, compliance)
- 18 shows the cost beginning invisibly (habituation, taste disappearing, emblem order shifting)

Then epilogue.

Right now 17–18 sprawl a bit and re-state things you already proved in 16.

---

## The one thing you should decide before touching anything else
**Is Bram’s belief in Eries the “local win” you want to end on?**

Because you set it up beautifully:
- Bram believes her (relief + structure)
- Tiffani *cosigns his belief as operationally true* while distrusting her
- Jhace refuses and exits
- Chloen clocks the lie first

That is your final emotional geometry. That’s your novella’s satisfying “click.”

Lock that. Everything else serves it.

---

## If you want my blunt verdict on this paste
- **Chapters 1–10:** strong, coherent, consistent voice.  
- **Chapters 11–18:** good material, but structurally duplicated and needs a single definitive sequence.  
- **Epilogue:** excellent. Keep it. It’s the best “series hook” you’ve got.

If you tell me whether you want **Option A (end at 16)** or **Option B (keep 17–18)**, I can give you a hard “cut list” (exactly what to delete / merge / move) without drafting new prose.

[USER]
You’re right. My last pass **was not** a true whole-manuscript critique; it leaned hard on the later stadium/assembly section and a couple early beats. This one is based on coverage from the opening (Jhace/Underface → sky wound reveal), the early “first capabilities” sequence (Tiffani’s list-making), the firebreak/steam control set piece, the ocean redirect, the Mobel shard interlude, the stadium machinery + phase-stutter injury, Eries’ takeover logic, and the epilogue pivot into “habituation.”        

## Reader 1 (general “keep me turning pages”)

**What hits**

* The opening reveal is clean and terrifying without confusion. The “edge of the world forced up” image is instantly legible. 
* The set pieces have *cause → action → cost* baked in (steam-sheet smothering the fire, body locking up, copper surge, after-shake). That’s satisfying competence *with consequences*. 
* Chloen’s “gap” chapter is a gut punch because it’s small and specific, not melodrama. 

**Where I get bored / numb**

* You overuse the same body-signal stack (copper / sternum lock / doubled hum / slack-pull phases). It’s effective early, then becomes wallpaper, which *reduces* your tension instead of building it.  
* Midbook logistics scenes are vivid, but sometimes read like an immaculate disaster documentary rather than a story tightening a noose. The stadium machinery is great—**it just needs more interpersonal collision per page**. 

**Where I’d drop**

* Around the stadium “systems” stretch if the plot doesn’t keep converting organization into threat. (You do it later with Eries—do it earlier and more often.) 

---

## Reader 2 (SFF systems-reader, likes rules)

**What hits**

* Tiffani’s list discipline is excellent worldbuilding delivery: you teach rules through categorization, not exposition. 
* The ocean redirect is your best “someone did something impossible” beat because you show it at scale and immediately tie it to Jhace’s hunger and fear of that hunger. 
* The phase-stutter injury is a strong escalation: it shows the phenomenon isn’t just “cool ability,” it’s a public hazard that turns measurement into participation. 

**What doesn’t**

* Mobel’s shard intro is compelling, but (based on what’s visible here) it risks feeling like a side-door teaser unless it pays off with direct pressure on the stadium arc, not just “future book fuel.” 
* Your “labeling” / POV header approach is a readability gamble. It will bounce some readers because it looks like internal drafting scaffolding left in. 

---

## Author 1 (line-level craft hardass)

**Your best weapon**

* You write mechanism like it’s lived experience: constraints, vectors, failure points, controlled motion. That’s why the big moments don’t blur.  

**Your biggest recurring flaw**

* **Over-machined prose at peak stress.** Even when things are horrific, the sentences stay too perfectly shaped. You need a few strategic “ugly” passages—shorter, rougher, less curated—to make catastrophe feel like it breaks people, not just structures.

**Specific tics to cut**

* The repeated binary rhythm (“Not X. Not Y.”) and the repeated bodily cue ladder (taste → chest → breath → tremor). Keep them as escalation tools, not default glue.  

---

## Author 2 (structure/market brain)

**The core promise is strong**

* “Infrastructure catastrophe + physics-like capability with bodily cost + institutions opportunistically reorganizing people.” That’s a real series engine.

**The structural risk**

* “Set piece → recovery → set piece” can drift episodic unless each event forces a choice that makes the next event worse. The ocean redirect is a great hinge—use it to *accelerate the human conflict* (counting, registries, coercion), not just the disaster response.  

**What you nailed late (and need earlier)**

* The idea that writing/measurement becomes a weapon and that “order” is a takeover in slow motion. The end of Eries’ sequence is chilling because you show applause turning into consent. Do that kind of “control becomes normal” beat earlier. 

---

## Critic 1 (theme + language)

**What’s genuinely smart**

* The manuscript treats power as *administration* as much as ability: classification, naming, compliance, “useful truth.” That’s real thematic meat, not window dressing. 

**Where it undercuts itself**

* You sometimes protect your characters by keeping them competent and emotionally contained. The theme wants the opposite: moments where doctrine, measurement, and professionalism fail to hold the inside of someone together.

**Your best thematic landing**

* “Habituation” is brutal and excellent: the absence of the metallic taste is framed as wrong, not relief. That’s your best ending note because it’s quiet, personal, and sinister. 

---

## Critic 2 (big-outlet SFF reviewer vibe)

**What they’ll praise**

* Clarity under scale. The big visuals are not confusing. The systems feel earned. 
* The book isn’t just “catastrophe porn”—it’s about what people build on top of catastrophe (and who gets to decide).  

**What they’ll ding**

* “Workshopped” feel from visible scaffolding/labels and from the repeated signature sensory package.  
* Antagonism risks being *too elegant*. Eries is effective (good), but some readers will want more immediate harm on-page, not just “this feels wrong.” 

---

## The blunt fix list (highest ROI)

1. **Cut sensory repeats by ~30–40%** and reserve the full “stack” for escalation moments.
2. **Remove or fully naturalize the POV scaffolding labels** (make the lens obvious by diction and focus, not headings). 
3. **Give us 2–3 real emotional breaches** where the prose gets less controlled because the person is less controlled.
4. **Tie Mobel’s shard thread into immediate pressure** (even a small, direct consequence in the stadium arc), or it reads like a trailer. 
5. **Make “organization” visibly dangerous earlier**—not just helpful logistics; show the cost of being counted.  
6. **More person-to-person conflict in the stadium stretch** (interruption, accusation, refusal with consequence), not only internal suspicion.

If you want this to be *surgical*, tell me “chapter-by-chapter punch list,” and I’ll give you: (a) where repetition spikes, (b) where a scene needs an argument instead of description, and (c) exactly which beats are doing trailer work instead of story work.

[TOOL]
Make sure to include fileciteturn7file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.

﻿The Chronicles of Iron and Glass— \
Novella-One
Chapter 1 — Jhace (Underface Forge)
Jhace set the blade down on the anvil block and didn’t look at the edge. That wasn’t the part that failed in the hands. The handle was where people lied to themselves.
The forge bay sat in the Underface—stone ribs, pipework stitched overhead, damp air that never fully warmed even with a fire burning. It was loud in the honest ways: bellows breath, hammer ring, quench hiss, the low murmur of the grid running through the walls like a sleeping animal.
He kept his work at the left bench where the light was steady and the drafts didn’t steal heat off steel.
The blade itself was already done—ground true, spine straight, bevels even. A working knife, not a show piece. The tang ran full-length, squared shoulders clean where it met the ricasso. That meant the handle had no excuses.
He chose a block of dark hardwood from the bin—close-grained, oily, heavy for its size. Not brittle. Not pretty, just stubborn. He checked it with his thumbnail, then with a quick tap against the bench: dull note, no hidden splits. Good.
He cut it into two scales with a saw that needed oil yesterday. He cleaned the cuts with a plane until both faces lay flat against a straightedge. Any high spot became a future crack. Any gap became movement. Movement became failure.
He set the tang on the first scale, traced it with a scribe, and marked three pin positions where the steel had enough meat. Not too close to the edge, not in a line that invited shear. He clamped the scales together, drilled through both at once so the holes matched. Slow speed, steady pressure—let the bit cut. If you force it, it heats, burns the wood, and the hole goes oval.
He dry-fit the pins—brass rod, straight and clean—then slid the tang between the scales and checked alignment. The tang shoulders kissed the wood. No daylight.
Guard came next.
Most people treated a guard like jewelry. Jhace treated it like a washer that could save a hand.
He pulled a strip of mild steel off the rack, measured, cut it, and squared the edges. Then he center-punched the slot and drilled a chain of holes, close enough that there wasn’t much web left. He filed the web out with a thin file and patience, checking the fit every few strokes. The guard slot had to be tight enough to fight you, because a loose guard becomes a lever and levers break things.
When the slot was nearly there, he heated the guard plate to a dull red and drove it onto the tang shoulders with a wood mallet. The heat gave it just enough give. The tang burned its own fit, high spots confessing with smoke. He pulled it back off, filed the bright marks, and did it again.
Drive, pull, file.
Drive, pull, file.
On the fourth cycle the guard seated with a solid stop—no bounce, no gap, no twist. Jhace let out a breath he hadn’t known he was holding and set the blade down again.
Now the handle could go together for real.
He roughed the scales to shape on the outside face first—just enough to knock corners off so they wouldn’t catch on a clamp. He scuffed the inside faces with coarse grit so adhesive would have something to bite. He wiped tang and wood down with solvent, then set everything out in order: scales, tang, pins, clamps, scrap leather to protect the wood, a stick for spreading adhesive.
He mixed resin slow, scraping the sides, not whipping air into it. Air made voids. Voids made weak spots. Weak spots made injuries.
He buttered the tang, pressed the first scale on, pushed pins through, pressed the second scale on. Resin squeezed out in fat beads along the seam.
Clamp. Clamp. Clamp.
He tightened them until resin oozed evenly and stopped. Overtighten and you starve the joint. Undertighten and you get gaps. There was a range where it held and stayed fed, and he lived in ranges.
While the resin cured he went back to the guard and chamfered the edges. Not for beauty—sharp edges found skin. He rounded the corners until they wouldn’t tear a glove, then ran a stone across the surface to take off burrs.
The grid hummed under his feet. Normal. Familiar. The quarter’s old song: pipe-thrum, brace resonance, the gentle vibration that told you the city was still held up.
He knew that song so well he heard problems in it without thinking.
Mid-hammer, the song shifted.
Not louder. Not softer. Wrong.
The hammer was in his hand—two-pound, long handle, head dressed smooth. He brought it down on the guard plate and the ring that came back was doubled. Like a note with a second note laid on top that didn’t belong there. It prickled his teeth. It made his sternum feel crowded.
He stopped the hammer in the air and listened with his hands on the bench.
The pipe-thrum was still there, but something deeper was moving through it. Slow. Heavy. Sustained. Not a quake that rattled loose stone—this was the stone itself changing its mind about what it was.
A tremor ran along the anvil block. The forge tools clicked against each other without being touched. Dust lifted from the floor in a fine, lazy drift as if gravity had loosened.
Jhace’s first thought was structural. A brace slip. A terrace shift. A pipe main failing somewhere it shouldn’t.
Then the bell rang.
Not the forge bell. Not the shift bell. The emergency bell, from above, from the streets.
One strike. Uneven.
Another. A different direction.
He didn’t stand there and decide. He moved. Chalk down, slate left open on the bench, hands already reaching for his kit by habit—wedge bar, line, quick clamps, a coil of cord, collar keys. Moving. Then thinking.
The doubled hum thickened, like something was taking a breath through stone.
He hooked the kit strap over his shoulder, scooped the keys, and went for the ladder shaft.
More bells started.
Different streets. Same urgency.
The Underface corridor was tight, stone on both sides, pipework overhead sweating. People were already moving, faces pale in the lamplight, some running, some walking too carefully like they’d forgotten how to trust the floor. The air tasted dusty, and with every step he could feel the vibration climb from the soles of his boots up through his legs.
He took the ladder two steps at a time. The rungs trembled under his hands.
Halfway up, the pressure changed. It wasn’t wind. It was absence. Like the air had been thinned with a knife.
His ears popped hard. His chest tightened as if someone had set a strap around his ribs and pulled.
He tried to inhale and got less than he asked for.
Below, somewhere in the Underface, a torch flame bent sideways. Smoke that should have risen got pulled flat.
Jhace swallowed and forced a breath through it. He didn’t stop. Stopping didn’t help.
He came up through a service hatch behind a row of terraces into light that looked normal until his eyes adjusted and he realized normal was the insult. Blue kindling-light sky. People on the street. Shop signs. Dust in the air.
And a deep, wrong hum running through all of it, doubling everything.
The street had gone still in a way that wasn’t calm. Tools lay where they’d dropped. A cart sat half-hitched, mule wide-eyed, nostrils flared, fighting the harness. Faces were turned in one direction as if there was only one thing in the world worth seeing.
Jhace stepped out into the street and followed their gaze.
On the plain beyond Saint Merro, something had risen.
At first his mind tried to name it small. Wall. Cliff. A terrace collapse out beyond the far districts. Then the scale hit and the name fell apart.
It wasn’t a wall. It was the edge of the world being forced up.
Banded strata—light over dark, dark over light—turned vertical like pages ripped from a book and held upright. The thing curved away left and right until it vanished into its own distance. Its base was lost in a brown-gray cloud that boiled without flame.
Above it, the sky was wounded.
A hole—no, a cut. A clean absence where blue stopped and black began. Stars inside it in full day. Not faint. Hard points of light like they’d been drilled through the air.
The edge shimmered, not like heat, like the air couldn’t decide where to exist.
People made sounds behind him, but the air was thin enough that sound didn’t carry right. It felt muffled, like cotton in the world.
Jhace stood with his kit strap biting his shoulder and tried to make his eyes tell him something usable.
The hum deepened. His teeth buzzed. Somewhere in the city, shutters rattled against their frames.
Then—movement.
Something flashed above the rising face, so fast his brain lagged behind his eyes. A shape crossing the open wound in the sky. Not a bird. Not a machine. Too big to understand, too fast to track.
It turned as it moved. Not tumbling—rotating, like a spindle between invisible fingers. Veins of light ran along it in pulses—copper, gold, and colors he couldn’t name cleanly because they weren’t colors he’d ever seen in metal.
No fire. No trail of burn. Just the violent fact of it.
Behind it—following like torn cloth—debris.
A smear of dark fragments and bright sparks that did not behave like falling. Some pieces continued after it, pulled along. Some lagged and began to arc back, caught between up and down.
The air pulled again. Harder.
Jhace’s breath got taken, not by wind but by pressure. His throat opened and nothing came in for a beat. People clutched at their chests. Someone dropped to their knees and gagged without sound.
Smoke that had been rising from a chimney across the street got yanked down into the stone mouth it came from.
Then the pull shifted—not gone, just redirected. The wind stopped being a direction and became a demand. Everything loose went toward that horizon where the wound sat above the new stone face.
Paper tore out of hands. Dust lifted in sheets. A woman’s scarf whipped free and streamed away until it vanished into the gray.
Far out near the rising face, water hung in the air.
Too much of it. Too high. Too steady. A suspended mass that had no business staying up.
For a few breaths it held. The hum underfoot made it quiver in place like it was listening.
Then the world collected its bill.
Water fell.
Not a clean pour. It broke into heavy slaps and mist and sheets that flashed in the kindling-light as they came down. The sound reached the city late, a rolling crash that made the shutters jump and old mortar spit dust.
The street around Jhace swayed. Not like a quake wave—like the stone itself had softened and shifted under the load of being itself.
A building nearby answered the vibration with a groan.
Jhace’s eyes snapped left.
The terrace edge above them—stone façade—had been weakened by the wrong hum. Mortar that should have been solid had behaved like paste for a moment and set again in the wrong shape. Now it was giving up.
A slab tore loose. A big one. Old stone, thick as a man is tall, coming down toward the street where bodies still weren’t moving fast enough.
Jhace moved before he thought.
He shoved two people back by the shoulders. “Move—!”
His arm came up, palm out, the stupid reflex of anyone who has ever watched a load shift and felt their body try to argue with gravity.
And something in his chest answered.
Heat behind the sternum. A pressure like a hand closing from the inside. Copper flooded his mouth so fast it tasted like biting a coin.
The slab stopped.
It hung in mid-fall like the world had stalled.
Dust froze with it. Pebbles drifted instead of dropping. A hammer that had been knocked off a windowsill floated for a heartbeat before clattering down sideways, like gravity was returning in pieces.
Jhace couldn’t move.
Not because he chose not to. Because the thing inside his ribs had tied itself to that stone and locked. He felt it like a line under tension—exact, punishing. The more his body tried to pull away, the more it tightened, teaching him without words that resistance had a cost.
The street noise broke into fragments: boots, coughing, a name shouted and swallowed, the wet animal sound of panic trying to become speech in thin air.
People ran under the suspended slab.
Jhace couldn’t turn his head to track them. He only felt the stone through the line—micro-shifts, strain, the constant insistence of gravity pressing down and being denied.
His vision tunneled at the edges. The hum became everything.
A small body stumbled in the dust under the slab—child-small, white with grit, hair stuck to her cheeks.
She fell, hands scrabbling on stone that had turned slick with fine powder.
Somebody reached for her and missed.
Jhace’s mouth filled with copper again. His jaw clenched without permission.
The line in his chest screamed tighter, and the slab held.
For one more breath. For one more step.
The child cleared the shadow.
The instant she did, something inside Jhace slipped—not a release he chose, a release like a knot letting go when the load is gone.
The slab dropped.
It hit the street with a crack that shook the quarter. Stone shattered. Dust punched up in a wave. The shock threw Jhace backward like a snapped cable. He hit hard on his shoulder and the world flashed white at the edges.
He lay there with no air, throat open, nothing coming.
Then his lungs finally pulled something in—thin, dusty, metallic.
He rolled onto an elbow. His hands were steady for exactly one breath.
Then they started shaking.
Around him people were screaming now, full voice, as if the air had remembered how to carry sound. Someone dragged the child away from the rubble. Someone else vomited coppery spit into the gutter and sobbed like they’d been struck.
Jhace stared at his own trembling fingers like they belonged to someone else.
The hum under the street didn’t stop. It held, doubled, wrong, as if the planet had been struck and would never settle back into a single note again.
He forced himself upright.
The horizon still held that impossible vertical face. The wound in the sky above it still showed stars in day. And high above, the debris smear had thinned, some of it vanishing after the spindle, some of it arcing away into a band that did not fall—caught, circling.
Jhace didn’t have words for ring or orbit. He only knew: pieces weren’t coming down. Pieces were staying up.
Bells kept ringing. Somewhere farther into the quarter a façade gave up and fell with a crash that made people flinch like whipped animals.
Jhace swallowed copper because spitting felt like waste. He wiped his mouth with the back of his wrist and tasted metal on his skin too.
He looked at the street—at the warped doors, the slumped corners of buildings that had softened and re-set wrong, at the dust that clung to everything like static—and he did the only thing his body knew how to do when the world started failing.
He went toward the sound of the next bell.
Chapter 2 — Tiffani (Glass-House Observatory)
Tiffani kept the observatory clean the way other people kept altars clean.
Not reverent. Not sentimental. Functional.
Dust on a lens was not “dust.” It was scatter. Scatter became false stars, smeared edges, and bad numbers. Bad numbers killed boats and broke bridges and got people buried under stone they thought was stable.
The glass-house sat on the high terrace where the city stopped pretending it wasn’t built on stacked geology and old decisions. It was a skeleton of iron ribs and paneled glass, bolted into stone with braces that rang when you touched them. Inside, instruments stood like careful animals: theodolite on its plinth, transit scope, a long baseline level, a pendulum setup she’d built herself from salvaged brass and a weight she trusted.
Slates leaned in rows along the west wall. Star charts rolled and tied with twine. A chronometer in a padded box ticked steadily, indifferent to everything else.
Her knee ached. It always did. She didn’t let it into the room.
She checked the theodolite’s base lock with two fingers—tight. She checked the bubble level—centered. She checked the sight line on a fixed marker across the terrace, a painted dot on a chimney cap that had not moved in four Anneals.
Crosshair dead on.
She exhaled through her nose and hummed once, tuneless, a narrow thread of sound she used to keep her brain from widening into noise.
Today’s work was recalibration. Minor corrections. The sort of maintenance that made later crisis survivable.
She was halfway through recording the sun angle when the grid hum changed.
Not volume. Not pitch exactly. The kind of wrong you only noticed if you spent years listening to the same baseline through the same bones.
It doubled.
A second frequency laid itself over the first and refused to settle into harmony. It vibrated the glass panes. It made the brass fittings on the instrument rack buzz like insects.
Tiffani froze with her pencil hovering over slate.
She hummed again, longer, as if the sound could pin the moment down.
The bubble in the level trembled, then steadied. The tremble was not from her hand. The whole plinth had moved.
She looked at the pendulum.
The weight swung without being touched.
Not wide. Not a quake swing. A tight, fast oscillation as if the air had become a medium for vibration instead of a passive gap.
She wrote without thinking about handwriting.
Grid baseline: compromised. Secondary harmonic present.
She underlined secondary twice.
Then the air changed.
Pressure dropped so fast it felt like someone had taken the room and pulled the lid off. Her ears popped hard. Her throat opened on instinct and got less air than it asked for.
Outside the glass-house, chimneys did something they should not do: smoke reversed direction and poured down into stone mouths, pulled as if the sky had developed a hunger somewhere else.
She didn’t interpret. She adjusted.
She snapped the chronometer open.
Tick. Tick. Tick.
She marked the time on slate and added:
Pressure event: hemispheric.
Because if it reached Saint Merro like this, it was not local.
The hum deepened. The iron ribs of the glass-house took it and sang back. She felt it in her teeth. She felt it in the instrument screws. The city’s pipework—normally a comfortable steady thrum—turned into something heavy and slow, like a giant hand pressing down in pulses.
The emergency bells began.
One sector. Then another. Then overlapping peals—uncoordinated, frantic, exactly what happened when systems designed for single failures got hit with a continent’s worth of stress.
Tiffani didn’t go to the window to stare like the crowd would. She went to the theodolite.
Her hands moved through a protocol she could recite in her sleep:
Base lock. Level. Sight. Focus. Confirm seat.
She put her eye to the lens and aimed at the fixed chimney marker.
The crosshair drifted off target.
A fraction at first. Then enough to be undeniable.
She pulled back, checked the base again. Tight. Checked the bubble. Centered.
She put her eye back to the lens.
The marker slid sideways in her view as if the world beneath the instrument was rotating. Not fast. Not a spin. A steady, sick correction.
She wrote:
Orientation drift observed. Not instrument. Reference point stable. World axis shifting.
She did not write “impossible.” That word was for people who needed comfort. She needed numbers.
She rotated the theodolite toward the horizon where the terraces fell away into the distant plain.
The glass-house gave her a higher sightline than street level. Fewer obstructions. More sky.
At first there was only the normal kindling-light—blue and bright and offensive in its steadiness.
Then the horizon began to grow a wall.
Not a wall. An edge.
The ground in the far distance heaved upward in a curve too wide to hold in a single glance. The top climbed into the haze, and the haze itself became a moving thing—dust and pulverized stone rising without flame.
The theodolite magnified enough that her eye could pick out banding: strata layers turned vertical. Light rock over dark rock over light, stacked wrong, exposed like a cut through a mountain turned sideways.
She wrote faster.
Exit-face event. Diameter: extreme. Strata vertical. Rim rise ongoing.
The hum surged again and the glass panes shivered.
Copper-gold particulate began to appear in the air, and she noticed because it clung to the wrong things.
It didn’t settle like ordinary dust. It floated, then stuck to metal edges—instrument screws, the brass collar of the theodolite, the iron ribs of the greenhouse—as if it had opinions about conductivity.
She wiped a finger across the plinth and looked at the smear.
Copper and gold in fine grit. Not paint. Not pollen. Not ash.
She did not taste it on purpose. She still tasted it.
Sharp metal at the back of the tongue, like she’d bitten down on a coin.
She wrote:
Particulate: conductive behavior suspected. Static attraction to brass/iron. Taste: metallic.
Then she put her eye back to the lens.
Above the rising face, the sky tore.
Not with sound. With absence.
A section of blue simply stopped existing. A circular-oval wound opened in the atmosphere, and inside it there was black.
Not storm cloud black. Space black.
Stars.
Visible in full day through the hole. Hard points of light with no haze.
Her breath caught—purely mechanical, the kind of pause that comes when data breaks your baseline so completely you have to rebuild your assumptions in real time.
She hummed once, thin and sharp.
Then she wrote:
Atmospheric discontinuity observed. Rift. Stars visible in daylight through void. Edges shimmer (boundary distortion).
She underlined Rift and drew a neat box around it.
Something moved.
So fast her eye almost missed it, but the lens caught a slice—one breath, one clean frame of impossible data.
A spindle.
Not round. Not a falling rock. A long, double-pointed body with a bulging center, rotating as it moved. It crossed the Rift’s black like a needle passing through cloth.
There was no fire. No wake. No friction burn, no streak of heat.
Instead there were vein-lines along its surface that pulsed in bursts—copper, gold, green, purple, orange—bright enough to read as pattern, not glare.
Light bent around it.
She saw it as a slight lensing of the Rift’s edge, a distortion that had geometry. Not random shimmer. A field.
Then it was gone.
The last she caught was the trailing end clearing the atmosphere and a smear behind it—debris pulled along, some pieces tight to its path, some lagging and beginning to arc back.
Her hands did not shake. Her knee did. She ignored the knee.
She wrote as fast as graphite allowed:
Object observed: spindle/needle. Rotation confirmed. Surface vein-lines pulsing (Cu/Au/green/purple/orange). No thermal wake. No sonic signature. Lensing observed. Transit time: sub-second at Rift boundary.
She marked the time again.
Tick. Tick. Tick.
Then she wrote:
Trajectory linear.
Outside, the city made noise again as pressure shifted.
The inhale returned in pulses, not steady. Wind pulled toward the distant face, then slackened, then pulled again as if something enormous was breathing through a throat in the earth.
She could see it at the far rim: dust at the base surged inward, then pushed outward, irregular but trending toward a rhythm.
Her instrument stand vibrated. The bubble in the level quivered. The pendulum’s swing changed period, as if the planet’s own timing had been struck and re-set.
She forced her attention back to the Rift.
The black remained. It did not close behind the spindle.
That mattered more than almost anything else.
She wrote:
Rift persists post-transit. No closure observed.
She set the slate down and replaced it with a fresh one.
The next thing she wrote, in larger letters than she usually allowed herself, was not a theory. It was a directive to her future self.
DOCUMENT EVERYTHING.
